\[
B_{125}=\{51,\dots,150\},\quad
B_{250}=\{151,\dots,250\}.
\]

For all other \(n\), choose disjoint \(B_n\) of size \(\varphi(n)\) using numbers \(>250\), and define
\[
A_n=\bigcup_{d\mid n} B_d.
\]

Then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|
=\sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)}\varphi(d)
=\gcd(i,j),
\]
and the above maxima are attained.

So the minimum is indeed

\[
\boxed{499}.
\]

Let  
\[
D=\{1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250\}.
\]

We want the minimum of
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d.
\]

## 1. Structural facts

From
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j),
\]
taking \(i=j\) gives
\[
|A_n|=n.
\]

Also, if \(i\mid j\), then \(\gcd(i,j)=i\), so
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=|A_i|,
\]
hence
\[
A_i\subseteq A_j.
\]

So for divisors of \(250\), the sets are ordered by divisibility.

---

## 2. Decompose \(A_{250}\) into disjoint “types”

Every element \(x\in A_{250}\) belongs to some of the sets \(A_d\) (\(d\mid 250\)). Because membership is upward-closed under divisibility and closed under gcd, \(x\) has a unique minimal divisor \(m\mid 250\) such that
\[
x\in A_d \iff m\mid d.
\]

Let \(B_m\) be the set of elements of minimal divisor \(m\). Then the \(B_m\) are disjoint, and for every \(d\mid 250\),
\[
A_d=\bigsqcup_{m\mid d} B_m.
\]

Let \(b_m=|B_m|\). Then
\[
\sum_{m\mid d} b_m=|A_d|=d.
\]
By Möbius inversion,
\[
b_m=\varphi(m).
\]

So for the divisors of \(250\), the block sizes are

\[
\begin{array}{c|cccccccc}
m&1&2&5&10&25&50&125&250\\ \hline
\varphi(m)&1&1&4&4&20&20&100&100
\end{array}
\]

---

## 3. Reduce to ordering these 8 blocks

Only the labels of the elements matter for maxima. In an optimal arrangement, we may place each block \(B_m\) as one consecutive interval, so the problem becomes: order these 8 blocks on the number line.

If \(m\mid d\), then putting \(B_d\) before \(B_m\) is never better: swapping them can only decrease \(\max A_m\) and does not increase any relevant maximum. Thus in an optimal order, blocks respect divisibility.

So in an optimal order, block \(B_d\) appears after all \(B_m\) with \(m\mid d\), and therefore
\[
\max A_d=\text{completion position of block }B_d.
\]

Hence we need a topological order of the divisibility poset minimizing the sum of completion times.

---

## 4. Best topological order

Among incomparable available blocks, putting the smaller block first is better, since if adjacent blocks have sizes \(p\le q\), then
\[
p+ (p+q) \le q+(q+p).
\]
So we should always place the smallest available block first.

Using sizes
\[
1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100
\]
with divisibility constraints, the optimal order is
\[
1,\ 2,\ 5,\ 10,\ 25,\ 50,\ 125,\ 250.
\]

Their completion positions are:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\max A_1&=1,\\
\max A_2&=1+1=2,\\
\max A_5&=2+4=6,\\
\max A_{10}&=6+4=10,\\
\max A_{25}&=10+20=30,\\
\max A_{50}&=30+20=50,\\
\max A_{125}&=50+100=150,\\
\max A_{250}&=150+100=250.
\end{aligned}
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d
=1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

---

## 5. Achievability

Take disjoint blocks:
\[
\begin{aligned}
B_1&=\{1\},\\
B_2&=\{2\},\\
B_5&=\{3,4,5,6\},\\
B_{10}&=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
B_{25}&=\{11,\dots,30\},\\
B_{50}&=\{31,\dots,50\},\\
B_{125}&=\{51,\dots,150\},\\
B_{250}&=\{151,\dots,250\},
\end{aligned}
\]
and define
\[
A_n=\bigcup_{d\mid n} B_d
\]
for all \(n\) (extending with disjoint blocks of size \(\varphi(d)\) for all other \(d\)). Then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)}\varphi(d)=\gcd(i,j),
\]
so the condition is satisfied, and the above maxima occur.

Thus the minimum is

\[
\boxed{499}.
\]

Let  
\[
g(i,j)=|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j).
\]

We want the minimum possible value of
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d,
\]
where the divisors of \(250\) are
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]

---

## 1. First structural facts

Since
\[
|A_n|=|A_n\cap A_n|=\gcd(n,n)=n,
\]
we have
\[
|A_n|=n.
\]

Also, if \(g=\gcd(i,j)\), then \(g\mid i\) and \(g\mid j\), so
\[
A_g\subseteq A_i,\qquad A_g\subseteq A_j
\]
because
\[
|A_g\cap A_i|=\gcd(g,i)=g=|A_g|.
\]
Thus \(A_g\subseteq A_i\cap A_j\). But both sets have size \(g\), since
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)=g=|A_g|.
\]
Therefore
\[
A_i\cap A_j=A_{\gcd(i,j)}.
\]

In particular, if \(d\mid n\), then
\[
A_d=A_d\cap A_n\subseteq A_n.
\]

So the sets are nested by divisibility.

---

## 2. Decompose into disjoint blocks

Define \(B_n\) to be the set of elements that first appear in \(A_n\), i.e.
\[
B_n=A_n\setminus \bigcup_{\substack{d\mid n\\ d<n}}A_d.
\]
Then the \(B_n\) are disjoint, and
\[
A_n=\bigsqcup_{d\mid n} B_d.
\]
Hence
\[
n=|A_n|=\sum_{d\mid n}|B_d|.
\]
By Möbius inversion,
\[
|B_n|=\varphi(n).
\]

So any valid construction is equivalent to choosing disjoint sets \(B_n\) with
\[
|B_n|=\varphi(n),\qquad A_n=\bigcup_{d\mid n}B_d.
\]

For the divisors of \(250\),
\[
250=2\cdot 5^3,
\]
and the relevant block sizes are:

\[
\begin{array}{c|cccccccc}
n & 1&2&5&10&25&50&125&250\\ \hline
\varphi(n)&1&1&4&4&20&20&100&100
\end{array}
\]

These sum to
\[
1+1+4+4+20+20+100+100=250.
\]

---

## 3. Reduce to ordering these 8 blocks

Only the blocks \(B_d\) with \(d\mid 250\) affect the sets \(A_d\) for \(d\mid 250\). Since \(A_{250}\) has size \(250\), we must have
\[
\max A_{250}\ge 250,
\]
and to minimize, we should use exactly the integers \(1,2,\dots,250\) for these 250 elements.

Now let \(L_d\) be the largest label used in block \(B_d\). Then
\[
\max A_n=\max_{d\mid n}L_d.
\]

We may assume the labels of each block are consecutive and the blocks appear one after another in increasing order of their largest labels: replacing a scattered arrangement by consecutive blocks in the same order does not increase any \(L_d\). So the problem becomes:

- arrange the 8 blocks in some order,
- each block \(B_d\) has length \(\varphi(d)\),
- minimize the sum of the completion positions corresponding to \(A_d\).

---

## 4. The block order must respect divisibility

If \(a\mid b\) but block \(B_b\) appears before \(B_a\), swap them.

- Any \(A_n\) not containing \(B_a\): unchanged.
- Any \(A_n\) containing both \(B_a\) and \(B_b\): its maximum stays the same, since the later of the two blocks still ends at the same place.
- Any \(A_n\) containing \(B_a\) but not \(B_b\): its maximum can only decrease.

So swapping cannot increase the objective.

Thus an optimal order can be assumed to satisfy:

\[
a\mid b \implies B_a \text{ comes before } B_b.
\]

In such an order, for each divisor \(d\mid 250\), all proper divisors of \(d\) occur before \(B_d\), so
\[
\max A_d = \text{completion time of block }B_d.
\]

Hence we now want to minimize the sum of completion times of these 8 jobs, with precedence given by divisibility.

---

## 5. Among incomparable blocks, shorter should come first

If two consecutive blocks \(X,Y\) are incomparable in divisibility, with lengths \(p_X>p_Y\), swapping them preserves feasibility. The total of their completion times changes from
\[
(C+p_X)+(C+p_X+p_Y)=2C+2p_X+p_Y
\]
to
\[
(C+p_Y)+(C+p_Y+p_X)=2C+2p_Y+p_X,
\]
which is smaller by \(p_X-p_Y>0\).

So in an optimal topological order, among available incomparable blocks, shorter blocks come first.

Starting from the precedence constraints, this forces the order

\[
B_1,\ B_2,\ B_5,\ B_{10},\ B_{25},\ B_{50},\ B_{125},\ B_{250}.
\]

Their lengths are
\[
1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100.
\]

So the completion times are

\[
\begin{aligned}
C_1&=1,\\
C_2&=1+1=2,\\
C_5&=2+4=6,\\
C_{10}&=6+4=10,\\
C_{25}&=10+20=30,\\
C_{50}&=30+20=50,\\
C_{125}&=50+100=150,\\
C_{250}&=150+100=250.
\end{aligned}
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d
=
1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250
=499.
\]

This is achievable, for example, by taking
\[
\begin{aligned}
B_1&=\{1\},\\
B_2&=\{2\},\\
B_5&=\{3,4,5,6\},\\
B_{10}&=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
B_{25}&=\{11,\dots,30\},\\
B_{50}&=\{31,\dots,50\},\\
B_{125}&=\{51,\dots,150\},\\
B_{250}&=\{151,\dots,250\},
\end{aligned}
\]
and assigning all other \(B_n\) disjoint numbers \(>250\).

So the minimum is

\[
\boxed{499}.
\]

Let  
\[
m_d=\max A_d \qquad (d\mid 250).
\]

We want the minimum possible value of
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} m_d.
\]

The divisors of \(250=2\cdot 5^3\) are
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]

## 1. Basic structure of the sets

Since
\[
|A_i\cap A_i|=|A_i|=\gcd(i,i)=i,
\]
we get
\[
|A_i|=i.
\]

Also, if \(d\mid n\), then
\[
|A_d\cap A_n|=\gcd(d,n)=d=|A_d|,
\]
so \(A_d\subseteq A_n\).

Thus whenever \(g=\gcd(i,j)\), we have \(A_g\subseteq A_i\cap A_j\), and both sets have size \(g\), so
\[
A_i\cap A_j=A_{\gcd(i,j)}.
\]

This is the standard “gcd family.”

---

## 2. A useful lower bound

We use the fact that if finite sets \(X,Y\subseteq \{1,2,\dots,M\}\), then
\[
|X\cup Y|\le M.
\]
So for sets \(A_r,A_s\),
\[
\max(m_r,m_s)\ge |A_r\cup A_s|.
\]

### Pair 1: \(A_2\) and \(A_5\)

\[
|A_2\cup A_5|=|A_2|+|A_5|-|A_2\cap A_5|
=2+5-\gcd(2,5)=2+5-1=6.
\]
Hence
\[
\max(m_2,m_5)\ge 6.
\]
Since \(m_2\ge 2\), we get
\[
m_2+m_5\ge 8.
\]

### Pair 2: \(A_{10}\) and \(A_{25}\)

\[
|A_{10}\cup A_{25}|=10+25-\gcd(10,25)=10+25-5=30,
\]
so
\[
\max(m_{10},m_{25})\ge 30.
\]
Since \(m_{10}\ge 10\),
\[
m_{10}+m_{25}\ge 40.
\]

### Pair 3: \(A_{50}\) and \(A_{125}\)

\[
|A_{50}\cup A_{125}|=50+125-\gcd(50,125)=50+125-25=150,
\]
so
\[
\max(m_{50},m_{125})\ge 150.
\]
Since \(m_{50}\ge 50\),
\[
m_{50}+m_{125}\ge 200.
\]

Also,
\[
m_1\ge 1,\qquad m_{250}\ge 250.
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} m_d
= m_1+(m_2+m_5)+(m_{10}+m_{25})+(m_{50}+m_{125})+m_{250}
\ge 1+8+40+200+250=499.
\]

So the sum is at least \(499\).

---

## 3. Construction achieving 499

Now we show \(499\) is attainable.

Take disjoint blocks \(B_n\) with
\[
|B_n|=\varphi(n),
\]
and define
\[
A_k=\bigcup_{d\mid k} B_d.
\]
Then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)} \varphi(d)=\gcd(i,j),
\]
so the condition is satisfied.

For the divisors of \(250\), use these blocks:

\[
|B_1|=1,\ |B_2|=1,\ |B_5|=4,\ |B_{10}|=4,\ |B_{25}|=20,\ |B_{50}|=20,\ |B_{125}|=100,\ |B_{250}|=100.
\]

Assign them consecutive integers in this order:
\[
B_1,B_2,B_5,B_{10},B_{25},B_{50},B_{125},B_{250}.
\]

So their largest elements are at positions
\[
1,\ 2,\ 6,\ 10,\ 30,\ 50,\ 150,\ 250.
\]

Then:
- \(A_1=B_1\), so \(m_1=1\),
- \(A_2=B_1\cup B_2\), so \(m_2=2\),
- \(A_5=B_1\cup B_5\), so \(m_5=6\),
- \(A_{10}=B_1\cup B_2\cup B_5\cup B_{10}\), so \(m_{10}=10\),
- \(A_{25}=B_1\cup B_5\cup B_{25}\), so \(m_{25}=30\),
- \(A_{50}=B_1\cup B_2\cup B_5\cup B_{10}\cup B_{25}\cup B_{50}\), so \(m_{50}=50\),
- \(A_{125}=B_1\cup B_5\cup B_{25}\cup B_{125}\), so \(m_{125}=150\),
- \(A_{250}\) contains all these blocks, so \(m_{250}=250\).

Thus
\[
1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

Since we already proved no smaller value is possible, this is the minimum.

\[
\boxed{499}
\]

Let  
\[
D=\{d:d\mid 250\}=\{1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250\}.
\]

We want to minimize
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d.
\]

## 1. Basic consequences of the condition

We are given
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\qquad \forall i,j\ge1.
\]

Taking \(i=j\), we get
\[
|A_i|=i.
\]

Also, if \(d\mid n\), then
\[
|A_d\cap A_n|=\gcd(d,n)=d=|A_d|,
\]
so \(A_d\subseteq A_n\).

Hence the sets are nested along divisibility.

---

## 2. Decompose elements by their “first appearance”

For each element \(x\), let \(m(x)\) be the smallest positive integer such that \(x\in A_{m(x)}\).

If \(m=m(x)\), then:

- since \(A_m\subseteq A_n\) whenever \(m\mid n\), we have \(x\in A_n\) for all multiples \(n\) of \(m\);
- conversely, if \(x\in A_n\), then \(x\in A_m\cap A_n=A_{\gcd(m,n)}\), and by minimality of \(m\), this forces \(\gcd(m,n)=m\), so \(m\mid n\).

Thus each element belongs to exactly those \(A_n\) with \(m(x)\mid n\).

Let
\[
B_m=\{x:m(x)=m\}.
\]
Then the \(B_m\) are disjoint and
\[
A_n=\bigsqcup_{m\mid n} B_m.
\]

So
\[
|A_n|=\sum_{m\mid n}|B_m|=n.
\]
By the standard identity \(\sum_{m\mid n}\varphi(m)=n\), we get
\[
|B_m|=\varphi(m).
\]

So for divisors of \(250\), the relevant block sizes are:

\[
\begin{array}{c|cccccccc}
m & 1 & 2 & 5 & 10 & 25 & 50 & 125 & 250\\ \hline
\varphi(m) & 1 & 1 & 4 & 4 & 20 & 20 & 100 & 100
\end{array}
\]

---

## 3. Reformulate as an ordering problem

All elements that matter for the sum lie in \(A_{250}\), which has size \(250\). So we may label those elements by \(1,2,\dots,250\).

Each block \(B_m\) has \(\varphi(m)\) elements, and
\[
A_d=\bigsqcup_{m\mid d}B_m.
\]

To minimize maxima, we should place these blocks as early as possible.

Also, if \(m\mid n\), then every set containing \(B_n\) also contains \(B_m\). So placing any element of \(B_n\) before an element of \(B_m\) can only hurt, never help. Hence in an optimal arrangement, blocks respect divisibility:
\[
m\mid n \implies B_m \text{ comes before } B_n.
\]

Thus we need a linear extension of the divisibility poset on \(D\).

If a block \(B_d\) is completed at position \(C_d\), then since all proper divisors of \(d\) come earlier, the largest label in \(A_d\) is exactly \(C_d\). So we must minimize
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} C_d.
\]

This is now a scheduling problem with processing times \(\varphi(d)\).

---

## 4. Find the best order

The divisibility relations force:

- \(1\) first.
- After \(1\), we may choose \(2\) or \(5\). Since \(\varphi(2)=1<4=\varphi(5)\), putting \(2\) first is better.
- Then \(5\) must come.
- After \(1,2,5\), we may choose \(10\) or \(25\). Since \(\varphi(10)=4<20=\varphi(25)\), put \(10\) first.
- Then \(25\).
- After that, choose between \(50\) and \(125\). Since \(20<100\), put \(50\) first.
- Finally \(250\).

So the optimal order is
\[
1,\ 2,\ 5,\ 10,\ 25,\ 50,\ 125,\ 250.
\]

---

## 5. Compute the completion times

Using block sizes \(1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100\):

\[
\begin{aligned}
C_1&=1,\\
C_2&=1+1=2,\\
C_5&=1+1+4=6,\\
C_{10}&=1+1+4+4=10,\\
C_{25}&=10+20=30,\\
C_{50}&=30+20=50,\\
C_{125}&=50+100=150,\\
C_{250}&=150+100=250.
\end{aligned}
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d
=
1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250
=499.
\]

---

## 6. Achievability

Take
\[
\begin{aligned}
B_1&=\{1\},\\
B_2&=\{2\},\\
B_5&=\{3,4,5,6\},\\
B_{10}&=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
B_{25}&=\{11,\dots,30\},\\
B_{50}&=\{31,\dots,50\},\\
B_{125}&=\{51,\dots,150\},\\
B_{250}&=\{151,\dots,250\},
\end{aligned}
\]
and define \(A_n=\bigsqcup_{m\mid n}B_m\) (with the remaining \(B_m\) for \(m\nmid 250\) chosen disjointly beyond \(250\), of sizes \(\varphi(m)\)). Then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)
\]
holds, and the above maxima are attained.

So the minimum possible value is

\[
\boxed{499}.
\]

Let  
\[
S=\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d
\]
and we want the minimum possible value of \(S\).

## 1. Basic structure of the sets

Since
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j),
\]
taking \(i=j\) gives
\[
|A_i|=i.
\]

Now if \(d\mid n\), then \(\gcd(d,n)=d\), so
\[
|A_d\cap A_n|=d=|A_d|.
\]
Hence
\[
A_d\subseteq A_n \qquad (d\mid n).
\]

So for divisors of \(250\), the sets are nested by divisibility.

---

## 2. Decompose \(A_{250}\) into disjoint blocks

The divisors of \(250\) are
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]

For each divisor \(t\mid 250\), let \(B_t\) be the set of elements whose *smallest* \(A_d\) (with \(d\mid 250\)) containing them is \(A_t\). Then the \(B_t\)'s are disjoint, and for every \(d\mid 250\),
\[
A_d=\bigsqcup_{t\mid d} B_t.
\]

Thus
\[
|A_d|=d=\sum_{t\mid d}|B_t|.
\]
By Möbius inversion,
\[
|B_t|=\varphi(t).
\]

So the block sizes are:
\[
\begin{aligned}
|B_1|&=\varphi(1)=1,\\
|B_2|&=\varphi(2)=1,\\
|B_5|&=\varphi(5)=4,\\
|B_{10}|&=\varphi(10)=4,\\
|B_{25}|&=\varphi(25)=20,\\
|B_{50}|&=\varphi(50)=20,\\
|B_{125}|&=\varphi(125)=100,\\
|B_{250}|&=\varphi(250)=100.
\end{aligned}
\]

These sum to \(250\), as expected.

---

## 3. Turn the problem into an ordering problem

All elements of all \(A_d\) with \(d\mid 250\) lie in \(A_{250}\), which has size \(250\). To minimize maxima, we should use the labels
\[
1,2,\dots,250
\]
for the elements of \(A_{250}\).

Now think of placing the blocks \(B_t\) in some order along these labels.

If \(a\mid b\), then every set containing \(B_b\) also contains \(B_a\). So if an element of \(B_b\) appears before an element of \(B_a\), swapping them cannot increase any \(\max A_d\), and may decrease some. Therefore in an optimal arrangement, all of \(B_a\) comes before all of \(B_b\).

So the blocks must be ordered respecting divisibility:
\[
1\prec 2\prec 10\prec 50\prec 250,
\qquad
1\prec 5\prec 25\prec 125\prec 250,
\]
with also \(5\prec 10\) and \(25\prec 50\).

Thus we are scheduling jobs of lengths \(\varphi(t)\) with these precedence constraints, minimizing the sum of completion times. Here the completion time of block \(B_d\) is exactly \(\max A_d\).

---

## 4. Find the optimal order

Among two available incomparable blocks, the shorter one should go first: if jobs of lengths \(p<q\) are scheduled as \(q,p\), swapping them to \(p,q\) reduces the sum of their completion times by \(q-p>0\).

So at each stage, take the shortest available block.

Available sequence:

- first \(B_1\) (forced),
- then \(B_2\) and \(B_5\) are available; choose \(B_2\) since \(1<4\),
- then \(B_5\),
- then \(B_{10}\) and \(B_{25}\); choose \(B_{10}\) since \(4<20\),
- then \(B_{25}\),
- then \(B_{50}\) and \(B_{125}\); choose \(B_{50}\) since \(20<100\),
- then \(B_{125}\),
- then \(B_{250}\).

So the optimal order is
\[
B_1,\ B_2,\ B_5,\ B_{10},\ B_{25},\ B_{50},\ B_{125},\ B_{250}.
\]

---

## 5. Compute the maxima

Using consecutive labels in that order:

- \(B_1\): size \(1\)  \(\Rightarrow \max A_1=1\),
- \(B_2\): size \(1\)  \(\Rightarrow \max A_2=2\),
- \(B_5\): size \(4\)  \(\Rightarrow \max A_5=6\),
- \(B_{10}\): size \(4\) \(\Rightarrow \max A_{10}=10\),
- \(B_{25}\): size \(20\) \(\Rightarrow \max A_{25}=30\),
- \(B_{50}\): size \(20\) \(\Rightarrow \max A_{50}=50\),
- \(B_{125}\): size \(100\) \(\Rightarrow \max A_{125}=150\),
- \(B_{250}\): size \(100\) \(\Rightarrow \max A_{250}=250\).

Therefore
\[
S=1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

This is achievable, for example by assigning:
\[
\begin{aligned}
B_1&=\{1\},\\
B_2&=\{2\},\\
B_5&=\{3,4,5,6\},\\
B_{10}&=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
B_{25}&=\{11,\dots,30\},\\
B_{50}&=\{31,\dots,50\},\\
B_{125}&=\{51,\dots,150\},\\
B_{250}&=\{151,\dots,250\},
\end{aligned}
\]
and for all other \(n\), use fresh disjoint blocks of size \(\varphi(n)\).

So the minimum possible value is

\[
\boxed{499}.
\]

Let  
\[
D=\{1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250\}.
\]

We will show the minimum is \(499\).

---

### 1. Structural decomposition of the family

Since \(i\mid j\) implies
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)=i=|A_i|,
\]
we get
\[
A_i\subseteq A_j \qquad (i\mid j).
\]

Also, because \(A_{\gcd(i,j)}\subseteq A_i\cap A_j\) and both sets have size \(\gcd(i,j)\), we actually have
\[
A_i\cap A_j=A_{\gcd(i,j)}.
\]

So every element \(x\) belongs to all \(A_n\) with \(n\) divisible by some minimal positive integer \(d\). Let \(B_d\) be the set of elements whose minimal index is \(d\). Then the \(B_d\) are disjoint and
\[
A_n=\bigsqcup_{d\mid n} B_d.
\]

Taking sizes:
\[
n=|A_n|=\sum_{d\mid n}|B_d|.
\]
By Möbius inversion,
\[
|B_d|=\varphi(d).
\]

For the divisors of \(250\), the relevant block sizes are:
\[
\varphi(1)=1,\quad \varphi(2)=1,\quad \varphi(5)=4,\quad \varphi(10)=4,
\]
\[
\varphi(25)=20,\quad \varphi(50)=20,\quad \varphi(125)=100,\quad \varphi(250)=100.
\]

---

### 2. Reformulate as an ordering problem

Only the blocks \(B_d\) for \(d\mid 250\) affect the sum. Their total size is
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\varphi(d)=250.
\]
So to minimize maxima, we should use exactly the integers \(1,2,\dots,250\) for these 250 elements.

Think of each block \(B_d\) as a job of length \(\varphi(d)\). If the blocks are placed consecutively in some order, let \(C_d\) be the finishing time of block \(B_d\).

Then
\[
\max A_n=\max_{d\mid n} C_d.
\]

---

### 3. Necessary order relations

If \(a\mid b\), then every \(A_n\) containing \(B_b\) also contains \(B_a\). Hence putting \(B_b\) before \(B_a\) can never help; swapping them does not increase any \(\max A_n\), and may decrease some. Thus in an optimal arrangement,
\[
a\mid b \implies B_a \text{ comes before } B_b.
\]

So among our divisors, the order must respect divisibility.

Also, \(B_{250}\) appears only in \(A_{250}\), whose maximum is always \(250\), so \(B_{250}\) should be last.

Thus we need a linear extension of divisibility on
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]

Among currently available incomparable blocks, shorter should go first, since for two adjacent jobs of lengths \(p\le q\), placing the \(p\)-job first gives smaller sum of completion times.

So the optimal order is:
\[
B_1,\ B_2,\ B_5,\ B_{10},\ B_{25},\ B_{50},\ B_{125},\ B_{250}.
\]

---

### 4. Compute the maxima

Using block lengths
\[
1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100,
\]
the finishing times are:
\[
1,\ 2,\ 6,\ 10,\ 30,\ 50,\ 150,\ 250.
\]

Because of the divisibility order, for each \(d\in D\), the last block contributing to \(A_d\) is exactly \(B_d\). Hence
\[
\max A_1=1,\quad
\max A_2=2,\quad
\max A_5=6,\quad
\max A_{10}=10,
\]
\[
\max A_{25}=30,\quad
\max A_{50}=50,\quad
\max A_{125}=150,\quad
\max A_{250}=250.
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d
=1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

---

### 5. Achievability

Take
\[
B_1=\{1\},\quad B_2=\{2\},\quad B_5=\{3,4,5,6\},\quad B_{10}=\{7,8,9,10\},
\]
\[
B_{25}=\{11,\dots,30\},\quad B_{50}=\{31,\dots,50\},
\]
\[
B_{125}=\{51,\dots,150\},\quad B_{250}=\{151,\dots,250\},
\]
and for all other \(d\), choose disjoint blocks \(B_d\) of size \(\varphi(d)\) using integers \(>250\). Then define
\[
A_n=\bigsqcup_{d\mid n} B_d.
\]
This satisfies \(|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\), and gives the sum \(499\).

So the minimum possible value is

\[
\boxed{499}.
\]

Let  
\[
m_d=\max A_d \qquad (d\mid 250).
\]

We want the minimum possible value of
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} m_d
=
m_1+m_2+m_5+m_{10}+m_{25}+m_{50}+m_{125}+m_{250}.
\]

## 1. Basic structure of the sets

Since
\[
|A_i\cap A_i|=|A_i|=\gcd(i,i)=i,
\]
we get
\[
|A_i|=i \quad \text{for every } i.
\]

Also, if \(i\mid j\), then \(\gcd(i,j)=i\), so
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=i=|A_i|.
\]
Hence \(A_i\subseteq A_j\).

So in particular,
\[
A_i\cap A_j=A_{\gcd(i,j)}.
\]

---

## 2. Lower bounds from unions

Because every element of \(A_d\) is at most \(m_d\), the union \(A_r\cup A_s\) is contained in \(\{1,2,\dots,\max(m_r,m_s)\}\). Thus
\[
|A_r\cup A_s|\le \max(m_r,m_s).
\]

We use this for three key pairs.

### Pair \(A_2, A_5\)
\[
|A_2\cup A_5|
=|A_2|+|A_5|-|A_2\cap A_5|
=2+5-\gcd(2,5)
=2+5-1=6.
\]
So
\[
\max(m_2,m_5)\ge 6.
\]
Since also \(m_2\ge 2\) and \(m_5\ge 5\), the minimum possible value of \(m_2+m_5\) is
\[
2+6=8.
\]

### Pair \(A_{10}, A_{25}\)
\[
|A_{10}\cup A_{25}|
=10+25-\gcd(10,25)
=10+25-5=30.
\]
So
\[
\max(m_{10},m_{25})\ge 30.
\]
With \(m_{10}\ge 10,\ m_{25}\ge 25\), the minimum possible value of
\[
m_{10}+m_{25}
\]
is
\[
10+30=40.
\]

### Pair \(A_{50}, A_{125}\)
\[
|A_{50}\cup A_{125}|
=50+125-\gcd(50,125)
=50+125-25=150.
\]
So
\[
\max(m_{50},m_{125})\ge 150.
\]
With \(m_{50}\ge 50,\ m_{125}\ge 125\), the minimum possible value of
\[
m_{50}+m_{125}
\]
is
\[
50+150=200.
\]

Also plainly,
\[
m_1\ge 1,\qquad m_{250}\ge 250.
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} m_d
\ge 1+8+40+200+250
=499.
\]

So the sum is at least \(499\).

---

## 3. Show that \(499\) is achievable

A standard construction is to use disjoint blocks \(E_n\) of size \(\varphi(n)\), and define
\[
A_k=\bigcup_{d\mid k} E_d.
\]
Then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|
=\sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)} |E_d|
=\sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)} \varphi(d)
=\gcd(i,j),
\]
as required.

For the divisors of \(250\), choose:

\[
\begin{aligned}
E_1&=\{1\},\\
E_2&=\{2\},\\
E_5&=\{3,4,5,6\},\\
E_{10}&=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
E_{25}&=\{11,12,\dots,30\},\\
E_{50}&=\{31,32,\dots,50\},\\
E_{125}&=\{51,52,\dots,150\},\\
E_{250}&=\{151,152,\dots,250\}.
\end{aligned}
\]
These have sizes
\[
1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100,
\]
which are exactly
\[
\varphi(1),\varphi(2),\varphi(5),\varphi(10),\varphi(25),\varphi(50),\varphi(125),\varphi(250).
\]

Then:
\[
\begin{aligned}
A_1&=E_1 &&\Rightarrow m_1=1,\\
A_2&=E_1\cup E_2 &&\Rightarrow m_2=2,\\
A_5&=E_1\cup E_5 &&\Rightarrow m_5=6,\\
A_{10}&=E_1\cup E_2\cup E_5\cup E_{10} &&\Rightarrow m_{10}=10,\\
A_{25}&=E_1\cup E_5\cup E_{25} &&\Rightarrow m_{25}=30,\\
A_{50}&=E_1\cup E_2\cup E_5\cup E_{10}\cup E_{25}\cup E_{50} &&\Rightarrow m_{50}=50,\\
A_{125}&=E_1\cup E_5\cup E_{25}\cup E_{125} &&\Rightarrow m_{125}=150,\\
A_{250}&=\bigcup_{d\mid 250}E_d &&\Rightarrow m_{250}=250.
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus
\[
1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

This matches the lower bound, so it is minimal.

\[
\boxed{499}
\]

Let the divisors of \(250\) be
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]

We are given
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\qquad \forall i,j\ge 1.
\]
In particular,
\[
|A_i|=|A_i\cap A_i|=i.
\]

## 1. Inclusion relations
If \(d\mid e\), then
\[
|A_d\cap A_e|=\gcd(d,e)=d=|A_d|,
\]
so \(A_d\subseteq A_e\).

Thus among the divisors of \(250\), the sets are nested according to divisibility.

## 2. The disjoint “new parts”
Define disjoint blocks corresponding to these divisors:
\[
\begin{aligned}
B_1&=A_1,\\
B_2&=A_2\setminus A_1,\\
B_5&=A_5\setminus A_1,\\
B_{10}&=A_{10}\setminus (A_2\cup A_5),\\
B_{25}&=A_{25}\setminus A_5,\\
B_{50}&=A_{50}\setminus (A_{10}\cup A_{25}),\\
B_{125}&=A_{125}\setminus A_{25},\\
B_{250}&=A_{250}\setminus (A_{50}\cup A_{125}).
\end{aligned}
\]
Using the intersection sizes, their cardinalities are
\[
\begin{aligned}
|B_1|&=1,\\
|B_2|&=2-1=1,\\
|B_5|&=5-1=4,\\
|B_{10}|&=10-(2+5-1)=4,\\
|B_{25}|&=25-5=20,\\
|B_{50}|&=50-(10+25-5)=20,\\
|B_{125}|&=125-25=100,\\
|B_{250}|&=250-(50+125-25)=100.
\end{aligned}
\]

So the block sizes are
\[
1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100.
\]

Also each \(A_d\) is the disjoint union of the \(B_e\) with \(e\mid d\).

---

## 3. Why divisibility order is optimal
Think of assigning positive integers to these blocks. Only the largest number in each block matters.

If a block \(B_f\) is placed before \(B_e\) where \(e\mid f\) and \(e\ne f\), swapping them cannot increase any \(\max A_d\): every set containing \(B_f\) also contains \(B_e\), while some sets contain \(B_e\) but not \(B_f\). So in an optimal arrangement, blocks appear in an order respecting divisibility.

In such an order, for each divisor \(d\), the block \(B_d\) is the last block appearing among those inside \(A_d\). Hence
\[
\max A_d=\text{(last position of }B_d\text{)}.
\]

So the problem becomes:

- schedule jobs \(1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250\),
- with processing times
  \[
  1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100,
  \]
- subject to divisibility precedence,
- minimizing the sum of completion times.

For two incomparable adjacent jobs, putting the shorter one first decreases the sum of completion times. Therefore the optimal linear extension is
\[
1,\ 2,\ 5,\ 10,\ 25,\ 50,\ 125,\ 250.
\]

Its completion times are the cumulative sums:
\[
1,\ 2,\ 6,\ 10,\ 30,\ 50,\ 150,\ 250.
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d
\ge 1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

---

## 4. Achievability
Now construct disjoint sets \(X_n\) with \(|X_n|=\varphi(n)\) for all \(n\), and for the divisors of \(250\) choose:
\[
\begin{aligned}
X_1&=\{1\},\\
X_2&=\{2\},\\
X_5&=\{3,4,5,6\},\\
X_{10}&=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
X_{25}&=\{11,\dots,30\},\\
X_{50}&=\{31,\dots,50\},\\
X_{125}&=\{51,\dots,150\},\\
X_{250}&=\{151,\dots,250\}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Put all other \(X_n\) disjointly afterward.

Define
\[
A_i=\bigcup_{d\mid i} X_d.
\]
Then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)}\varphi(d)=\gcd(i,j).
\]

For the divisors of \(250\), the maxima are exactly
\[
1,2,6,10,30,50,150,250,
\]
so the sum is \(499\).

Thus the minimum possible value is

\[
\boxed{499}.
\]

Let  
\[
M_d=\max A_d \qquad (d\mid 250).
\]
We want the minimum possible value of
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} M_d
= M_1+M_2+M_5+M_{10}+M_{25}+M_{50}+M_{125}+M_{250}.
\]

## 1. Basic facts

From the condition
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j),
\]
taking \(i=j\) gives
\[
|A_i|=i.
\]
So each \(A_i\) has exactly \(i\) elements.

Also, if \(i\mid j\), then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)=i=|A_i|,
\]
so \(A_i\subseteq A_j\).

---

## 2. Lower bounds

We will use the simple fact:

If a finite set \(S\) of positive integers has \(|S|=k\), then \(\max S\ge k\).

Also, if \(X,Y\) are sets of positive integers, then
\[
|X\cup Y|\le \max(\max X,\max Y).
\]
So a large union forces at least one of the maxima to be large.

---

### (a) \(M_1\)

Since \(|A_1|=1\),
\[
M_1\ge 1.
\]

---

### (b) \(M_2+M_5\)

We have
\[
|A_2|=2,\qquad |A_5|=5,\qquad |A_2\cap A_5|=\gcd(2,5)=1.
\]
Thus
\[
|A_2\cup A_5|=2+5-1=6.
\]
Hence
\[
\max(M_2,M_5)\ge 6.
\]
Also \(M_2\ge 2\) and \(M_5\ge 5\). Therefore the smallest possible sum is
\[
M_2+M_5\ge 2+6=8.
\]

---

### (c) \(M_{10}+M_{25}\)

Similarly,
\[
|A_{10}|=10,\qquad |A_{25}|=25,\qquad |A_{10}\cap A_{25}|=\gcd(10,25)=5.
\]
So
\[
|A_{10}\cup A_{25}|=10+25-5=30.
\]
Hence
\[
\max(M_{10},M_{25})\ge 30.
\]
With \(M_{10}\ge 10\) and \(M_{25}\ge 25\), we get
\[
M_{10}+M_{25}\ge 10+30=40.
\]

---

### (d) \(M_{50}+M_{125}\)

Likewise,
\[
|A_{50}|=50,\qquad |A_{125}|=125,\qquad |A_{50}\cap A_{125}|=\gcd(50,125)=25,
\]
so
\[
|A_{50}\cup A_{125}|=50+125-25=150.
\]
Thus
\[
\max(M_{50},M_{125})\ge 150.
\]
Since \(M_{50}\ge 50\) and \(M_{125}\ge 125\),
\[
M_{50}+M_{125}\ge 50+150=200.
\]

---

### (e) \(M_{250}\)

Since \(|A_{250}|=250\),
\[
M_{250}\ge 250.
\]

---

Adding all these lower bounds,
\[
M_1+(M_2+M_5)+(M_{10}+M_{25})+(M_{50}+M_{125})+M_{250}
\ge 1+8+40+200+250=499.
\]
So
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} M_d\ge 499.
\]

---

## 3. Construction achieving 499

Now we show 499 is attainable.

A standard construction is to choose pairwise disjoint sets \(B_n\) with
\[
|B_n|=\varphi(n),
\]
and define
\[
A_i=\bigcup_{n\mid i} B_n.
\]
Then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|
=\sum_{n\mid \gcd(i,j)} \varphi(n)
=\gcd(i,j),
\]
since \(\sum_{d\mid m}\varphi(d)=m\).

For the divisors of \(250\), assign:

\[
\begin{aligned}
B_1&=\{1\},\\
B_2&=\{2\},\\
B_5&=\{3,4,5,6\},\\
B_{10}&=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
B_{25}&=\{11,12,\dots,30\},\\
B_{50}&=\{31,32,\dots,50\},\\
B_{125}&=\{51,52,\dots,150\},\\
B_{250}&=\{151,152,\dots,250\}.
\end{aligned}
\]
These sizes are
\[
1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100,
\]
which are exactly
\[
\varphi(1),\varphi(2),\varphi(5),\varphi(10),\varphi(25),\varphi(50),\varphi(125),\varphi(250).
\]

Then:

- \(A_1=B_1\), so \(M_1=1\).
- \(A_2=B_1\cup B_2\), so \(M_2=2\).
- \(A_5=B_1\cup B_5\), so \(M_5=6\).
- \(A_{10}=B_1\cup B_2\cup B_5\cup B_{10}\), so \(M_{10}=10\).
- \(A_{25}=B_1\cup B_5\cup B_{25}\), so \(M_{25}=30\).
- \(A_{50}=B_1\cup B_2\cup B_5\cup B_{10}\cup B_{25}\cup B_{50}\), so \(M_{50}=50\).
- \(A_{125}=B_1\cup B_5\cup B_{25}\cup B_{125}\), so \(M_{125}=150\).
- \(A_{250}\) is the union of all eight, so \(M_{250}=250\).

Thus
\[
1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

This matches the lower bound, so it is minimal.

\[
\boxed{499}
\]

Let  
\[
S=\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d.
\]
The divisors of \(250=2\cdot 5^3\) are
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]

We will find a lower bound for \(S\), then construct sets achieving it.

---

### 1. Basic facts

Since
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j),
\]
taking \(i=j\) gives
\[
|A_i|=i.
\]

Also, if \(d\mid n\), then \(\gcd(d,n)=d\), so
\[
|A_d\cap A_n|=d=|A_d|.
\]
Hence
\[
A_d\subseteq A_n.
\]

---

### 2. A useful lower-bound trick

For any finite set \(X\) of positive integers,
\[
\max X\ge |X|.
\]
Also, for two finite sets \(X,Y\),
\[
\max(\max X,\max Y)\ge |X\cup Y|.
\]

So for two sets \(X,Y\), one of \(\max X,\max Y\) is at least \(|X\cup Y|\), and the other is at least the smaller of \(|X|,|Y|\). Thus
\[
\max X+\max Y\ge |X\cup Y|+\min(|X|,|Y|).
\]

We apply this to three pairs.

---

### 3. Lower bounds for the needed terms

Let \(m_d=\max A_d\).

#### (i) \(m_1\)

Since \(A_1\) is nonempty and contains positive integers,
\[
m_1\ge 1.
\]

#### (ii) \(m_2+m_5\)

We have
\[
|A_2|=2,\qquad |A_5|=5,\qquad |A_2\cap A_5|=\gcd(2,5)=1.
\]
So
\[
|A_2\cup A_5|=2+5-1=6.
\]
Hence
\[
m_2+m_5\ge 2+6=8.
\]

#### (iii) \(m_{10}+m_{25}\)

Similarly,
\[
|A_{10}|=10,\quad |A_{25}|=25,\quad |A_{10}\cap A_{25}|=\gcd(10,25)=5,
\]
so
\[
|A_{10}\cup A_{25}|=10+25-5=30.
\]
Thus
\[
m_{10}+m_{25}\ge 10+30=40.
\]

#### (iv) \(m_{50}+m_{125}\)

Also,
\[
|A_{50}|=50,\quad |A_{125}|=125,\quad |A_{50}\cap A_{125}|=\gcd(50,125)=25,
\]
so
\[
|A_{50}\cup A_{125}|=50+125-25=150.
\]
Thus
\[
m_{50}+m_{125}\ge 50+150=200.
\]

#### (v) \(m_{250}\)

Since \(|A_{250}|=250\),
\[
m_{250}\ge 250.
\]

---

Adding all these:
\[
S \ge 1+8+40+200+250=499.
\]

So the minimum possible value is at least \(499\).

---

### 4. Construction attaining \(499\)

Use the standard totient-block construction.

Create pairwise disjoint sets \(B_n\) with
\[
|B_n|=\varphi(n)
\]
for every positive integer \(n\), and define
\[
A_m=\bigcup_{n\mid m} B_n.
\]
Then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|
= \sum_{n\mid \gcd(i,j)} \varphi(n)
= \gcd(i,j),
\]
using \(\sum_{d\mid k}\varphi(d)=k\).

Now choose the blocks for divisors of \(250\) as consecutive intervals:

\[
\begin{aligned}
B_1&=\{1\},\\
B_2&=\{2\},\\
B_5&=\{3,4,5,6\},\\
B_{10}&=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
B_{25}&=\{11,12,\dots,30\},\\
B_{50}&=\{31,32,\dots,50\},\\
B_{125}&=\{51,52,\dots,150\},\\
B_{250}&=\{151,152,\dots,250\}.
\end{aligned}
\]

(For all other \(n\), choose disjoint \(B_n\) using integers \(>250\).)

Then:

- \(A_1=B_1\), so \(\max A_1=1\).
- \(A_2=B_1\cup B_2\), so \(\max A_2=2\).
- \(A_5=B_1\cup B_5\), so \(\max A_5=6\).
- \(A_{10}=B_1\cup B_2\cup B_5\cup B_{10}\), so \(\max A_{10}=10\).
- \(A_{25}=B_1\cup B_5\cup B_{25}\), so \(\max A_{25}=30\).
- \(A_{50}=B_1\cup B_2\cup B_5\cup B_{10}\cup B_{25}\cup B_{50}\), so \(\max A_{50}=50\).
- \(A_{125}=B_1\cup B_5\cup B_{25}\cup B_{125}\), so \(\max A_{125}=150\).
- \(A_{250}\) contains exactly \(1,\dots,250\), so \(\max A_{250}=250\).

Therefore
\[
S=1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

This matches the lower bound, so it is minimal.

\[
\boxed{499}
\]

We first extract the structure forced by  
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\qquad(\forall i,j\ge1).
\]

## 1. Basic consequences

Taking \(i=j\), we get
\[
|A_i|=i.
\]

Also, if \(i\mid j\), then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)=i=|A_i|,
\]
so \(A_i\subseteq A_j\).

In fact,
\[
A_i\cap A_j=A_{\gcd(i,j)},
\]
because \(A_{\gcd(i,j)}\subseteq A_i\cap A_j\) and both have size \(\gcd(i,j)\).

---

## 2. Decompose elements by their “minimal index”

For each element \(x\) appearing in some \(A_n\), let \(m(x)\) be the smallest positive integer such that \(x\in A_{m(x)}\).

Claim:  
\[
x\in A_n \iff m(x)\mid n.
\]

- If \(m(x)\mid n\), then \(A_{m(x)}\subseteq A_n\), so \(x\in A_n\).
- If \(x\in A_n\), then \(x\in A_{m(x)}\cap A_n=A_{\gcd(m(x),n)}\). By minimality of \(m(x)\), this forces \(\gcd(m(x),n)=m(x)\), so \(m(x)\mid n\).

Thus the elements split into disjoint blocks
\[
E_m:=\{x:m(x)=m\},
\]
and
\[
A_n=\bigcup_{m\mid n} E_m.
\]

Let \(c_m=|E_m|\). Then
\[
|A_n|=\sum_{m\mid n} c_m=n.
\]
By Möbius inversion,
\[
c_m=\varphi(m).
\]

So there are exactly \(\varphi(m)\) elements of type \(m\).

---

## 3. The types relevant for divisors of \(250\)

Since \(250=2\cdot 5^3\), its divisors are
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]
For these \(m\), the block sizes are
\[
\varphi(1)=1,\ \varphi(2)=1,\ \varphi(5)=4,\ \varphi(10)=4,\ \varphi(25)=20,\ \varphi(50)=20,\ \varphi(125)=100,\ \varphi(250)=100.
\]

And for each divisor \(d\mid 250\),
\[
A_d=\bigcup_{m\mid d} E_m.
\]

Since \(A_d\subseteq A_{250}\) for all \(d\mid 250\), and \(|A_{250}|=250\), to minimize the maxima we should label the \(250\) elements of \(A_{250}\) with \(1,2,\dots,250\).

So the problem becomes: order these 8 blocks of sizes above to minimize the sum of the finishing times of the sets \(A_d\).

---

## 4. Why the blocks should respect divisibility

If \(e\mid f\), then every \(A_d\) containing block \(E_f\) also contains \(E_e\).  
So if \(E_f\) appears before \(E_e\), swapping them cannot increase any \(\max A_d\). Hence in an optimal arrangement we may assume

\[
E_e \text{ appears before } E_f \quad \text{whenever } e\mid f.
\]

Thus the block order is a linear extension of the divisibility poset on the 8 divisors of \(250\).

In such an order, the latest block needed for \(A_d\) is exactly \(E_d\) itself, so \(\max A_d\) is just the completion time of block \(E_d\).

Therefore we need to minimize
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} C_d,
\]
where job \(d\) has processing time \(\varphi(d)\), under the precedence \(e\prec f\) if \(e\mid f\).

---

## 5. Optimal order

Among available incomparable jobs, shorter processing time should go first.

Starting from the precedence constraints:

- \(1\) must be first.
- Then \(2\) and \(5\) are available; choose \(2\) first since \(1<4\).
- Then \(5\).
- Then \(10\) and \(25\) are available; choose \(10\) first since \(4<20\).
- Then \(25\).
- Then \(50\) and \(125\) are available; choose \(50\) first since \(20<100\).
- Then \(125\).
- Finally \(250\).

So the optimal block order is
\[
1,\ 2,\ 5,\ 10,\ 25,\ 50,\ 125,\ 250.
\]

Its cumulative completion times are:

\[
\begin{aligned}
C_1&=1,\\
C_2&=1+1=2,\\
C_5&=2+4=6,\\
C_{10}&=6+4=10,\\
C_{25}&=10+20=30,\\
C_{50}&=30+20=50,\\
C_{125}&=50+100=150,\\
C_{250}&=150+100=250.
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d
=1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250
=499.
\]

---

## 6. Achievability

Assign the blocks as:

- \(E_1=\{1\}\)
- \(E_2=\{2\}\)
- \(E_5=\{3,4,5,6\}\)
- \(E_{10}=\{7,8,9,10\}\)
- \(E_{25}=\{11,\dots,30\}\)
- \(E_{50}=\{31,\dots,50\}\)
- \(E_{125}=\{51,\dots,150\}\)
- \(E_{250}=\{151,\dots,250\}\)

and for all other \(m\), use disjoint unused integers in blocks of size \(\varphi(m)\). Then define
\[
A_n=\bigcup_{m\mid n}E_m.
\]
This satisfies \(|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\), and the desired sum is exactly \(499\).

\[
\boxed{499}
\]

Let  
\[
M_d=\max A_d \qquad (d\mid 250).
\]

We want to minimize
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} M_d
= M_1+M_2+M_5+M_{10}+M_{25}+M_{50}+M_{125}+M_{250}.
\]

## 1. Basic structure of the sets

Since
\[
|A_i\cap A_i|=|A_i|=\gcd(i,i)=i,
\]
we have
\[
|A_i|=i \quad \text{for all } i.
\]

Also, if \(i\mid j\), then \(\gcd(i,j)=i\), so
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=i=|A_i|.
\]
Because \(A_i\cap A_j\subseteq A_i\) and they have the same finite size, it follows that
\[
A_i\subseteq A_j \qquad (i\mid j).
\]

In particular, for the divisors of \(250\), we get all the expected containments.

## 2. Lower bounds from unions

Because every element of \(A_d\) is a positive integer at most \(M_d\), we have
\[
A_d\subseteq \{1,2,\dots,M_d\}.
\]
So for any two such sets,
\[
|A_a\cup A_b|\le \max(M_a,M_b).
\]

We now use this on some incomparable pairs.

### Pair \(A_2, A_5\)

\[
|A_2\cup A_5|
=|A_2|+|A_5|-|A_2\cap A_5|
=2+5-\gcd(2,5)=2+5-1=6.
\]
Hence
\[
\max(M_2,M_5)\ge 6.
\]
Also \(M_2\ge 2\), \(M_5\ge 5\). Therefore
\[
M_2+M_5\ge 8.
\]

### Pair \(A_{10}, A_{25}\)

\[
|A_{10}\cup A_{25}|
=10+25-\gcd(10,25)
=10+25-5
=30.
\]
So
\[
\max(M_{10},M_{25})\ge 30.
\]
Since \(M_{10}\ge 10\), \(M_{25}\ge 25\),
\[
M_{10}+M_{25}\ge 40.
\]

### Pair \(A_{50}, A_{125}\)

\[
|A_{50}\cup A_{125}|
=50+125-\gcd(50,125)
=50+125-25
=150.
\]
Thus
\[
\max(M_{50},M_{125})\ge 150.
\]
With \(M_{50}\ge 50\), \(M_{125}\ge 125\),
\[
M_{50}+M_{125}\ge 200.
\]

### Also
\[
M_1\ge 1,\qquad M_{250}\ge 250
\]
because \(|A_1|=1\) and \(|A_{250}|=250\).

So altogether,
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} M_d
\ge 1+8+40+200+250=499.
\]

## 3. Construction attaining 499

We now show 499 is achievable.

Take pairwise disjoint blocks \(B_n\) of size \(\varphi(n)\), and define
\[
A_m=\bigcup_{n\mid m} B_n.
\]
Then
\[
A_i\cap A_j=\bigcup_{n\mid \gcd(i,j)} B_n,
\]
so
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|
=\sum_{n\mid \gcd(i,j)} \varphi(n)
=\gcd(i,j),
\]
as required.

For the divisors of \(250\), assign:

\[
\begin{aligned}
B_1&=\{1\},\\
B_2&=\{2\},\\
B_5&=\{3,4,5,6\},\\
B_{10}&=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
B_{25}&=\{11,12,\dots,30\},\\
B_{50}&=\{31,32,\dots,50\},\\
B_{125}&=\{51,52,\dots,150\},\\
B_{250}&=\{151,152,\dots,250\}.
\end{aligned}
\]

(For all other \(n\), choose disjoint blocks \(B_n\) using integers \(>250\).)

Now compute:

- \(A_1=B_1\), so \(M_1=1\).
- \(A_2=B_1\cup B_2\), so \(M_2=2\).
- \(A_5=B_1\cup B_5\), so \(M_5=6\).
- \(A_{10}=B_1\cup B_2\cup B_5\cup B_{10}\), so \(M_{10}=10\).
- \(A_{25}=B_1\cup B_5\cup B_{25}\), so \(M_{25}=30\).
- \(A_{50}=B_1\cup B_2\cup B_5\cup B_{10}\cup B_{25}\cup B_{50}\), so \(M_{50}=50\).
- \(A_{125}=B_1\cup B_5\cup B_{25}\cup B_{125}\), so \(M_{125}=150\).
- \(A_{250}\) contains all these blocks, so \(M_{250}=250\).

Thus
\[
1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

So the minimum possible value is

\[
\boxed{499}.
\]

Let the divisors of \(250\) be
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]

We will determine the structure forced on the sets \(A_d\) for these divisors, then optimize their largest elements.

---

### 1. Basic consequences of the condition

Given
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j),
\]
taking \(i=j\) gives
\[
|A_i|=i.
\]

Also, if \(d\mid n\), then
\[
|A_d\cap A_n|=\gcd(d,n)=d=|A_d|,
\]
so necessarily
\[
A_d\subseteq A_n.
\]

Hence for divisors of \(250\), the family is nested by divisibility.

Moreover, for divisors \(d,e\mid 250\),
\[
A_{\gcd(d,e)}\subseteq A_d\cap A_e,
\]
and both sets have size \(\gcd(d,e)\), so
\[
A_d\cap A_e=A_{\gcd(d,e)}.
\]

---

### 2. Decompose \(A_{250}\) into disjoint blocks

For each \(x\in A_{250}\), among the divisors \(d\mid 250\) with \(x\in A_d\), there is a unique minimal one; call it \(m_x\).

Let
\[
B_m=\{x\in A_{250}: m_x=m\}\qquad (m\mid 250).
\]
Then the \(B_m\)'s are disjoint and
\[
A_d=\bigsqcup_{m\mid d} B_m.
\]

So if \(b_m=|B_m|\), then for every \(d\mid 250\),
\[
\sum_{m\mid d} b_m = |A_d|=d.
\]
By Möbius inversion,
\[
b_m=\varphi(m).
\]

Thus the block sizes are:

\[
\begin{array}{c|cccccccc}
m & 1&2&5&10&25&50&125&250\\ \hline
|B_m|=\varphi(m) & 1&1&4&4&20&20&100&100
\end{array}
\]

---

### 3. Reduce to an ordering problem

Only the labels of the elements matter now. Since \(A_{250}\) has \(250\) elements, the smallest possible choice is to label them by \(1,2,\dots,250\).

Think of each block \(B_m\) as a job of length \(\varphi(m)\). If the blocks are placed consecutively in some order, then for each divisor \(d\), \(\max A_d\) is the completion time of the last block among \(\{B_m:m\mid d\}\).

Also, if \(m\mid n\), then swapping \(B_n\) to come after \(B_m\) never increases any \(\max A_d\). So in an optimal arrangement, the blocks appear in an order respecting divisibility.

Therefore we need a topological order of the divisibility poset minimizing the sum of completion times.

The processing times are:
\[
1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100.
\]

---

### 4. Find the best topological order

A valid divisibility-respecting order must start with \(1\).

Now compare choices:

- After \(1\), available blocks are \(2\) and \(5\).
  - If we take \(2\) first, the best continuation is
    \[
    1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
    \]
  - If we take \(5\) first, the best continuation is
    \[
    1,5,2,10,25,50,125,250,
    \]
    which turns out worse.

Similarly, after \(1,2,5\), compare \(10\) and \(25\):
- \(10\) before \(25\) is better than \(25\) before \(10\).
And after \(25\), \(50\) before \(125\) is better than \(125\) before \(50\).

So the optimal order is
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]

Its completion times are:
\[
1,\ 2,\ 6,\ 10,\ 30,\ 50,\ 150,\ 250.
\]

Hence
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d
=
1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250
=499.
\]

---

### 5. Achievability

Take disjoint blocks of the required sizes:
\[
\begin{aligned}
B_1&=\{1\},\\
B_2&=\{2\},\\
B_5&=\{3,4,5,6\},\\
B_{10}&=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
B_{25}&=\{11,\dots,30\},\\
B_{50}&=\{31,\dots,50\},\\
B_{125}&=\{51,\dots,150\},\\
B_{250}&=\{151,\dots,250\}.
\end{aligned}
\]
For all other \(n\), choose disjoint blocks \(B_n\) of size \(\varphi(n)\) using integers \(>250\), and define
\[
A_k=\bigcup_{d\mid k} B_d.
\]
Then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|
=
\sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)}\varphi(d)
=
\gcd(i,j),
\]
so this is a valid global construction, and for \(d\mid 250\) the maxima are exactly the numbers above.

Thus the minimum is

\[
\boxed{499}
\]

Let \(X_i\) be the face showing on top on the \(i\)-th roll, for \(i=1,2,\dots,6\).

A sticker is visible at the end exactly when no later sticker was placed on that same face.

We want
\[
p=P(\text{exactly one blank face}\mid \text{stickers }2,4,6\text{ are visible}).
\]

---

## 1. Describe the conditioning event

Let \(E\) be the event that stickers \(2,4,6\) are visible at the end.

- Sticker \(6\) is always visible, since nothing comes after it.
- Sticker \(2\) is visible iff none of rolls \(3,4,5,6\) land on face \(X_2\).
- Sticker \(4\) is visible iff neither roll \(5\) nor \(6\) lands on face \(X_4\).

So \(E\) means:
- \(X_3,X_4,X_5,X_6\neq X_2\),
- \(X_5,X_6\neq X_4\).

In particular, \(X_2\neq X_4\).

---

## 2. Compute \(P(E)\)

Count choices roll by roll:

- \(X_1\): any of 6 faces
- \(X_2\): any of 6 faces
- \(X_3\): 5 choices (\(\neq X_2\))
- \(X_4\): 5 choices (\(\neq X_2\))
- \(X_5\): 4 choices (\(\neq X_2,X_4\))
- \(X_6\): 4 choices (\(\neq X_2,X_4\))

Thus
\[
P(E)=\frac{6\cdot 6\cdot 5\cdot 5\cdot 4\cdot 4}{6^6}
=\frac{25}{81}.
\]

---

## 3. Interpret “exactly one blank face”

At the end, exactly one face is blank means exactly 5 distinct faces were ever hit among \(X_1,\dots,X_6\).

Now condition on \(E\). By symmetry, we may fix
\[
X_2=1,\qquad X_4=2.
\]
Then:
- \(X_3\in\{2,3,4,5,6\}\),
- \(X_5,X_6\in\{3,4,5,6\}\),
- \(X_1\in\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}\).

Faces \(1\) and \(2\) are definitely used, so to have exactly 5 used faces total, among the four faces
\[
\{3,4,5,6\}
\]
exactly 3 must appear.

So we need the probability that among \(X_1,X_3,X_5,X_6\), exactly 3 of \(\{3,4,5,6\}\) occur.

---

## 4. Count favorable cases under \(E\)

Under \(E\), for fixed \(X_2=1, X_4=2\), there are
\[
6\cdot 5\cdot 4\cdot 4=480
\]
possible choices for \((X_1,X_3,X_5,X_6)\).

We split by whether \(X_5=X_6\).

### Case 1: \(X_5=X_6\)

There are 4 choices for this common face in \(\{3,4,5,6\}\).

Then only 1 of the 4 faces \(\{3,4,5,6\}\) has appeared so far, so \(X_1\) and \(X_3\) must contribute exactly 2 new distinct faces from the remaining 3.

- \(X_1\): 3 choices
- \(X_3\): 2 choices

So favorable sequences here:
\[
4\cdot 3\cdot 2=24.
\]

### Case 2: \(X_5\neq X_6\)

There are
\[
4\cdot 3=12
\]
ordered choices for \((X_5,X_6)\).

Now 2 of the 4 faces \(\{3,4,5,6\}\) have appeared, and we need exactly one of the remaining 2 to appear among \(X_1,X_3\).

For fixed distinct \(X_5,X_6\), let the missing two faces be \(a,b\).

Count \((X_1,X_3)\) pairs where exactly one of \(a,b\) appears.

- Total possibilities: \(6\cdot 5=30\).
- “\(a\) appears, \(b\) does not”:
  - \(X_1\in\{1,2,\text{used faces},a\}\): 5 options
  - \(X_3\in\{2,\text{used faces},a\}\): 4 options  
  total \(20\), but subtract those with no \(a\): \(4\cdot 3=12\), giving \(8\).
- Similarly, “\(b\) appears, \(a\) does not” gives \(8\).

So for fixed distinct \(X_5,X_6\), favorable \((X_1,X_3)\) pairs:
\[
8+8=16.
\]

Thus favorable sequences here:
\[
12\cdot 16=192.
\]

---

## 5. Conditional probability

Total favorable under \(E\):
\[
24+192=216.
\]

Therefore
\[
p=\frac{216}{480}=\frac{9}{20}.
\]

So \(m=9\), \(n=20\), and
\[
m+n=29.
\]

\[
\boxed{29}
\]

Let the top faces on the 6 rolls be \(X_1,X_2,\dots,X_6\), where each \(X_i\) is uniformly random from the 6 faces.

A sticker placed at time \(i\) is visible at the end exactly when that face is never rolled again afterward.

So the condition

- sticker \(2\) visible means \(X_2\notin\{X_3,X_4,X_5,X_6\}\),
- sticker \(4\) visible means \(X_4\notin\{X_5,X_6\}\),
- sticker \(6\) is always visible.

Let \(E\) be the event that all even-numbered stickers are visible.

Also, “exactly one face blank” means exactly 5 distinct faces were rolled among \(X_1,\dots,X_6\).

---

## 1. Count outcomes in \(E\)

We count sequences \((X_1,\dots,X_6)\):

- \(X_2\): \(6\) choices
- \(X_4\): must differ from \(X_2\), so \(5\) choices
- \(X_3\): cannot equal \(X_2\), so \(5\) choices
- \(X_5\): cannot equal \(X_2\) or \(X_4\), so \(4\) choices
- \(X_6\): cannot equal \(X_2\) or \(X_4\), so \(4\) choices
- \(X_1\): any face, so \(6\) choices

Thus
\[
|E|=6\cdot 5\cdot 5\cdot 4\cdot 4\cdot 6=14400.
\]

---

## 2. Count outcomes in \(E\) with exactly one blank face

Fix \(X_2=a\) and \(X_4=b\), with \(a\neq b\).  
There are \(6\cdot 5\) choices for \((a,b)\).

Let the other 4 faces be \(S\).  
To have exactly one blank face, exactly one of these 4 faces is never rolled. Choose that absent face in \(4\) ways.

So among the remaining 3 faces \(T\subset S\), all 3 must appear somewhere in \(X_1,X_3,X_5,X_6\).

Now under \(E\):

- \(X_1\in \{a,b\}\cup T\): \(5\) choices
- \(X_3\in \{b\}\cup T\): \(4\) choices
- \(X_5\in T\): \(3\) choices
- \(X_6\in T\): \(3\) choices

Total:
\[
5\cdot 4\cdot 3\cdot 3=180.
\]

We need all 3 faces in \(T\) to appear at least once. Use inclusion-exclusion:

- Missing a particular face of \(T\):
  \[
  4\cdot 3\cdot 2\cdot 2=48
  \]
  and there are \(3\) such faces.

- Missing two particular faces of \(T\):
  \[
  3\cdot 2\cdot 1\cdot 1=6
  \]
  and there are \(\binom32=3\) such pairs.

So valid assignments for \((X_1,X_3,X_5,X_6)\) are
\[
180-3(48)+3(6)=180-144+18=54.
\]

Therefore
\[
|E\cap\{\text{exactly one blank}\}|
=6\cdot 5\cdot 4\cdot 54
=6480.
\]

---

## 3. Compute the conditional probability

\[
p=\frac{6480}{14400}=\frac{9}{20}.
\]

Thus \(m=9\), \(n=20\), and
\[
m+n=29.
\]

\[
\boxed{29}
\]

Let \(X_i\) be the top face on the \(i\)-th roll, for \(i=1,2,\dots,6\).

Each sequence \((X_1,\dots,X_6)\) is equally likely, with probability \(6^{-6}\).

We want
\[
p=P(\text{exactly one face blank}\mid \text{stickers }2,4,6\text{ are visible}).
\]

So we count favorable sequences and divide by the total number satisfying the visibility condition.

---

## 1. Count sequences where stickers \(2,4,6\) are visible

A sticker is visible at the end iff no later sticker was placed on the same face.

Thus:

- Sticker \(2\) visible \(\iff X_2\neq X_3,X_4,X_5,X_6\).
- Sticker \(4\) visible \(\iff X_4\neq X_5,X_6\).
- Sticker \(6\) is always visible.

So:

- \(X_2\): \(6\) choices
- \(X_4\): must differ from \(X_2\), so \(5\) choices
- \(X_6\): must differ from \(X_2,X_4\), so \(4\) choices
- \(X_3\): must differ from \(X_2\), so \(5\) choices
- \(X_5\): must differ from \(X_2,X_4\), so \(4\) choices
- \(X_1\): any face, so \(6\) choices

Therefore
\[
N(E)=6\cdot 5\cdot 4\cdot 5\cdot 4\cdot 6=14400.
\]

---

## 2. Add the condition that exactly one face is blank

A face is blank at the end iff it was never landed on. So “exactly one face blank” means exactly \(5\) distinct faces appeared among \(X_1,\dots,X_6\).

Fix \(X_2=a,\ X_4=b,\ X_6=c\), where \(a,b,c\) are distinct faces.  
There are \(6\cdot 5\cdot 4\) ways to choose these.

Let the other three faces be \(d,e,f\).

Now:

- \(X_3\in\{b,c,d,e,f\}\)
- \(X_5\in\{c,d,e,f\}\)
- \(X_1\in\{a,b,c,d,e,f\}\)

Since \(a,b,c\) are already used, to have exactly \(5\) distinct faces total, among \(X_1,X_3,X_5\) we must use **exactly two** of \(d,e,f\).

### Count such triples \((X_1,X_3,X_5)\)

Choose which one of \(d,e,f\) is absent: \(3\) choices.

For a fixed absent face, say \(f\):

- \(X_1\in\{a,b,c,d,e\}\): \(5\) choices
- \(X_3\in\{b,c,d,e\}\): \(4\) choices
- \(X_5\in\{c,d,e\}\): \(3\) choices

Total avoiding \(f\):
\[
5\cdot 4\cdot 3=60.
\]

Now require both \(d\) and \(e\) to appear at least once.

- Missing \(d\): \(4\cdot 3\cdot 2=24\)
- Missing \(e\): \(4\cdot 3\cdot 2=24\)
- Missing both \(d,e\): \(3\cdot 2\cdot 1=6\)

By inclusion-exclusion:
\[
60-24-24+6=18.
\]

Since the absent face can be any of \(d,e,f\), total:
\[
3\cdot 18=54.
\]

So
\[
N(A\cap E)= (6\cdot 5\cdot 4)\cdot 54=6480.
\]

---

## 3. Conditional probability

\[
p=\frac{N(A\cap E)}{N(E)}=\frac{6480}{14400}=\frac{9}{20}.
\]

Thus \(m=9,\ n=20\), so
\[
m+n=29.
\]

\[
\boxed{29}
\]

Let the top faces rolled on the 6 rolls be \(x_1,x_2,\dots,x_6\), where each \(x_i\in\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}\).

Sticker \(i\) is placed on face \(x_i\). A sticker is visible at the end exactly when that face is never rolled again later.

We want
\[
p=P(\text{exactly one blank face}\mid \text{stickers }2,4,6\text{ are visible}).
\]

---

## 1. Count the outcomes where stickers \(2,4,6\) are visible

Let \(E\) be the event that stickers \(2,4,6\) are visible.

- Sticker \(2\) visible \(\iff x_2\notin\{x_3,x_4,x_5,x_6\}\).
- Sticker \(4\) visible \(\iff x_4\notin\{x_5,x_6\}\).
- Sticker \(6\) is always visible.

Count sequences \((x_1,\dots,x_6)\) satisfying \(E\):

- \(x_2\): \(6\) choices
- \(x_4\): cannot equal \(x_2\), so \(5\) choices
- \(x_6\): cannot equal \(x_2\) or \(x_4\), so \(4\) choices
- \(x_3\): cannot equal \(x_2\), so \(5\) choices
- \(x_5\): cannot equal \(x_2\) or \(x_4\), so \(4\) choices
- \(x_1\): \(6\) choices

Thus
\[
|E|=6\cdot 5\cdot 4\cdot 5\cdot 4\cdot 6=14400.
\]

---

## 2. Count the outcomes with exactly one blank face and with \(E\)

Exactly one blank face means exactly \(5\) distinct faces were ever rolled.

Since there are 6 rolls total, this means among \(x_1,\dots,x_6\):

- exactly one face appears twice,
- the other four used faces appear once each.

So there is exactly one pair of equal positions.

We now determine which position-pairs are allowed under \(E\):

- Since sticker \(2\) is visible, \(x_2\) cannot repeat later. So position \(2\) may only pair with position \(1\):  
  \((1,2)\) allowed.
- Since sticker \(4\) is visible, \(x_4\) cannot repeat later. So position \(4\) may pair only with \(1\) or \(3\):  
  \((1,4),(3,4)\) allowed.
- Position \(6\) may pair with earlier positions, except not with \(2\) or \(4\) (that would hide sticker \(2\) or \(4\)):  
  \((1,6),(3,6),(5,6)\) allowed.
- Pairs among odd positions are also allowed:  
  \((1,3),(1,5),(3,5)\).

So the allowed equal-position pairs are:
\[
(1,2),(1,3),(1,4),(1,5),(1,6),(3,4),(3,5),(3,6),(5,6),
\]
a total of \(9\) pairs.

For each allowed pair:

- choose the repeated face: \(6\) choices,
- choose the other 4 distinct faces for the remaining 4 positions: \(5\cdot 4\cdot 3\cdot 2=120\) choices.

So each allowed pair gives
\[
6\cdot 120=720
\]
valid sequences.

Hence
\[
|E\cap B|=9\cdot 720=6480,
\]
where \(B\) is the event “exactly one face is blank.”

---

## 3. Compute the conditional probability

\[
p=\frac{|E\cap B|}{|E|}=\frac{6480}{14400}=\frac{9}{20}.
\]

Thus \(m=9\) and \(n=20\), so
\[
m+n=29.
\]

\[
\boxed{29}
\]

Let \(X_i\) be the face showing on top on the \(i\)-th roll, for \(i=1,\dots,6\).

All \(6^6\) sequences \((X_1,\dots,X_6)\) are equally likely.

We want
\[
p=P(\text{exactly one blank face at end}\mid \text{stickers }2,4,6\text{ are visible}).
\]

So let

- \(A\): exactly one face is blank at the end,
- \(B\): stickers \(2,4,6\) are visible at the end.

Then
\[
p=\frac{P(A\cap B)}{P(B)}.
\]

---

## 1. Compute \(P(B)\)

Sticker \(2\) is visible iff the face rolled on roll 2 never appears again on rolls \(3,4,5,6\).

Sticker \(4\) is visible iff the face rolled on roll 4 never appears again on rolls \(5,6\).

Sticker \(6\) is always visible, since nothing comes after roll 6.

Count sequences satisfying \(B\):

- \(X_1\): any of 6 choices
- \(X_2\): any of 6 choices
- \(X_3\): must avoid \(X_2\), so 5 choices
- \(X_4\): must avoid \(X_2\), so 5 choices
- \(X_5\): must avoid both \(X_2\) and \(X_4\), so 4 choices
- \(X_6\): must avoid both \(X_2\) and \(X_4\), so 4 choices

Thus
\[
|B|=6\cdot 6\cdot 5\cdot 5\cdot 4\cdot 4=14400.
\]
So
\[
P(B)=\frac{14400}{6^6}=\frac{25}{81}.
\]

---

## 2. Compute \(P(A\cap B)\)

Event \(A\) means exactly one face is never rolled. Since there are 6 rolls total, that means exactly 5 distinct faces appear among \(X_1,\dots,X_6\).

So among the 6 rolls, exactly one face value is repeated once, and the other 4 face values each appear once.

Thus there is exactly one pair of positions \((i,j)\) with \(X_i=X_j\), and all other rolls are distinct.

### Which position-pairs are allowed under \(B\)?

Since sticker \(2\) must remain visible, the face at roll 2 cannot appear later.  
So if roll 2 is part of the repeated pair, it can only match roll 1. Thus the only pair involving 2 is
\[
(1,2).
\]

Since sticker \(4\) must remain visible, the face at roll 4 cannot appear later.  
So if roll 4 is part of the repeated pair, it can only match roll 1 or roll 3:
\[
(1,4),\ (3,4).
\]

All other repeated pairs not involving 2 or 4 later are allowed.

The full allowed list is:
\[
(1,2),(1,3),(1,4),(1,5),(1,6),(3,4),(3,5),(3,6),(5,6).
\]
That is \(9\) possible position-pairs.

### Count sequences for one fixed allowed pair

Fix one allowed repeated pair, say \((1,3)\). Then we need exactly 5 distinct faces total:

- choose the repeated face: 6 choices,
- choose the remaining 4 distinct faces in the other positions: \(5\cdot 4\cdot 3\cdot 2\) choices.

So for each allowed pair, the number of sequences is
\[
6\cdot 5\cdot 4\cdot 3\cdot 2=720.
\]

Hence
\[
|A\cap B|=9\cdot 720=6480.
\]
So
\[
P(A\cap B)=\frac{6480}{6^6}=\frac{5}{36}.
\]

---

## 3. Conditional probability

\[
p=\frac{P(A\cap B)}{P(B)}
=\frac{\frac{5}{36}}{\frac{25}{81}}
=\frac{5}{36}\cdot\frac{81}{25}
=\frac{9}{20}.
\]

Thus
\[
p=\frac{9}{20},
\]
so \(m=9\) and \(n=20\).

Therefore,
\[
m+n=29.
\]

\[
\boxed{29}
\]

Let \(X_i\) be the face on top when Joanne places sticker \(i\).  
Then \(X_1,X_2,\dots,X_6\) are independent and uniformly distributed over the 6 faces.

We want

\[
p=P(\text{exactly one blank face at end}\mid \text{all even stickers are visible}).
\]

---

### 1. Interpret the condition on even stickers

A sticker is visible at the end iff no later sticker is placed on the same face.

So:

- sticker \(2\) is visible iff \(X_2\notin\{X_3,X_4,X_5,X_6\}\),
- sticker \(4\) is visible iff \(X_4\notin\{X_5,X_6\}\),
- sticker \(6\) is always visible.

Thus the conditioning event \(E\) is:

- \(X_2\) is different from \(X_3,X_4,X_5,X_6\),
- \(X_4\) is different from \(X_5,X_6\).

---

### 2. Count outcomes satisfying \(E\)

Choose:

- \(X_2\): \(6\) ways,
- \(X_4\): \(5\) ways (must differ from \(X_2\)),
- \(X_3\): \(5\) ways (must differ from \(X_2\)),
- \(X_5\): \(4\) ways (must differ from \(X_2,X_4\)),
- \(X_6\): \(4\) ways (must differ from \(X_2,X_4\)),
- \(X_1\): \(6\) ways (anything).

So

\[
|E|=6\cdot 5\cdot 5\cdot 4\cdot 4\cdot 6=14400.
\]

---

### 3. Interpret “exactly one face left blank”

A face is blank iff it is never chosen.  
So “exactly one blank face” means exactly \(5\) distinct faces appear among \(X_1,\dots,X_6\).

Let \(B\) be the event that exactly \(5\) distinct faces are used.

We count \(E\cap B\).

---

### 4. Fix \(X_2=a\) and \(X_4=b\)

Under \(E\), \(a\neq b\).

Let the other 4 faces be \(c,d,e,f\).

Also:

- \(X_3\in\{b,c,d,e,f\}\),
- \(X_5,X_6\in\{c,d,e,f\}\),
- \(X_1\) is arbitrary.

We split into cases.

---

## Case 1: \(X_3=b\)

Then so far only faces \(a,b\) have appeared.

To end with exactly 5 distinct faces, the three remaining placements \(X_5,X_6,X_1\) must introduce exactly 3 new faces.

That means:

- \(X_5\) and \(X_6\) must be distinct faces in \(\{c,d,e,f\}\),
- \(X_1\) must be a third different face in \(\{c,d,e,f\}\).

Count:

\[
4\cdot 3\cdot 2=24.
\]

---

## Case 2: \(X_3\in\{c,d,e,f\}\)

Choose \(X_3=c\) first; there are 4 choices for which such face it is.

Now \(a,b,c\) are already used, so we need exactly 2 more distinct faces among \(X_5,X_6,X_1\).

The unused faces besides \(c\) are \(d,e,f\).  
We must use exactly 2 of these 3.

Choose which 2 new faces: \(\binom{3}{2}=3\).

For a fixed pair \(\{u,v\}\subset\{d,e,f\}\), count assignments of \((X_5,X_6,X_1)\) using only \(\{a,b,c,u,v\}\), with both \(u\) and \(v\) appearing at least once:

- \(X_5,X_6=(u,v)\) or \((v,u)\): \(2\) ways, then \(X_1\) has \(5\) choices \(\Rightarrow 10\)
- \((u,u)\): then \(X_1=v\) \(\Rightarrow 1\)
- \((v,v)\): then \(X_1=u\) \(\Rightarrow 1\)
- \((u,c)\) or \((c,u)\): then \(X_1=v\) \(\Rightarrow 2\)
- \((v,c)\) or \((c,v)\): then \(X_1=u\) \(\Rightarrow 2\)

Total per chosen pair:

\[
10+1+1+2+2=16.
\]

Thus for fixed \(c\),

\[
3\cdot 16=48.
\]

Since \(X_3\) could be any of 4 faces in \(\{c,d,e,f\}\),

\[
4\cdot 48=192.
\]

---

### 5. Total favorable count

For fixed \(a,b\):

\[
24+192=216.
\]

Now choose \(a=X_2\) and \(b=X_4\):

\[
|E\cap B|=6\cdot 5\cdot 216=6480.
\]

So

\[
p=\frac{|E\cap B|}{|E|}=\frac{6480}{14400}=\frac{9}{20}.
\]

Thus \(m=9\), \(n=20\), and

\[
m+n=29.
\]

\[
\boxed{29}
\]

Let \(X_i\) be the face showing on top on the \(i\)-th roll.

After roll \(i\), sticker \(i\) is placed on face \(X_i\).

Define:

- \(A\): exactly one face is blank at the end  
- \(C\): all even-numbered stickers are visible at the end

We want
\[
p=P(A\mid C)=\frac{P(A\cap C)}{P(C)}.
\]

## 1. Interpret the events

A face is blank at the end iff it was never rolled.  
So “exactly one face is blank” means exactly \(5\) distinct faces appear among \(X_1,\dots,X_6\).

Now for visibility:

- Sticker \(2\) is visible iff no later sticker is placed on the same face:
  \[
  X_2\notin \{X_3,X_4,X_5,X_6\}.
  \]
- Sticker \(4\) is visible iff
  \[
  X_4\notin \{X_5,X_6\}.
  \]
- Sticker \(6\) is always visible.

Thus
\[
C:\quad X_2\notin \{X_3,X_4,X_5,X_6\}\text{ and }X_4\notin \{X_5,X_6\}.
\]

---

## 2. Count outcomes for \(C\)

We count possibilities for \((X_2,X_3,X_4,X_5,X_6)\).  
Fix \(X_4=a\) first (\(6\) choices later).

We classify \((X_3,X_5,X_6)\) by the number
\[
d = |\{X_3,X_4,X_5,X_6\}|.
\]

Since \(X_4\neq X_5,X_6\):

### Case 1: \(X_5=X_6=b\neq a\)
There are \(5\) choices for \(b\).

- If \(X_3=a\) or \(b\): \(2\) choices, then \(d=2\).
- Otherwise: \(4\) choices, then \(d=3\).

So this gives:
- \(10\) tuples with \(d=2\),
- \(20\) tuples with \(d=3\).

### Case 2: \(X_5\neq X_6\), both different from \(a\)
There are \(5\cdot 4=20\) ordered choices for \((X_5,X_6)\).

- If \(X_3\in\{a,X_5,X_6\}\): \(3\) choices, then \(d=3\).
- Otherwise: \(3\) choices, then \(d=4\).

So this gives:
- \(60\) tuples with \(d=3\),
- \(60\) tuples with \(d=4\).

Hence for fixed \(a=X_4\):

- \(d=2\): \(10\) tuples
- \(d=3\): \(80\) tuples
- \(d=4\): \(60\) tuples

Now \(X_2\) must avoid all \(d\) used faces, so it has \(6-d\) choices.

Thus for fixed \(X_4=a\), number of valid \((X_2,\dots,X_6)\) is
\[
10(6-2)+80(6-3)+60(6-4)=10\cdot4+80\cdot3+60\cdot2=400.
\]

Since \(X_4\) has \(6\) choices, total valid \((X_2,\dots,X_6)\) is
\[
6\cdot 400=2400.
\]

Finally \(X_1\) is unrestricted for event \(C\), so total full sequences \((X_1,\dots,X_6)\) satisfying \(C\) is
\[
6\cdot 2400=14400.
\]

---

## 3. Count outcomes for \(A\cap C\)

Under \(C\), \(X_2\) is different from all of \(X_3,X_4,X_5,X_6\).  
So if \(d=|\{X_3,X_4,X_5,X_6\}|\), then among \(X_2,\dots,X_6\) there are \(d+1\) distinct faces.

We want exactly \(5\) distinct faces among all \(X_1,\dots,X_6\).

### If \(d=2\)
Then \(X_2,\dots,X_6\) use \(3\) distinct faces, so even with \(X_1\) we can reach at most \(4\).  
Impossible.

### If \(d=3\)
Then \(X_2,\dots,X_6\) use \(4\) distinct faces, so \(X_1\) must be a new face not among those \(4\).  
There are \(2\) choices for \(X_1\).

Number of such outcomes for fixed \(X_4=a\):
\[
80 \text{ tuples for }(X_3,X_5,X_6)\times 3 \text{ choices for }X_2 \times 2 \text{ choices for }X_1
=480.
\]

### If \(d=4\)
Then \(X_2,\dots,X_6\) already use \(5\) distinct faces, so \(X_1\) must repeat one of those \(5\) faces.  
There are \(5\) choices for \(X_1\).

Number for fixed \(X_4=a\):
\[
60 \times 2 \times 5 = 600.
\]

So for fixed \(X_4=a\), total for \(A\cap C\) is
\[
480+600=1080.
\]

With \(6\) choices for \(X_4\), total full sequences for \(A\cap C\) is
\[
6\cdot1080=6480.
\]

---

## 4. Compute the conditional probability

\[
p=\frac{P(A\cap C)}{P(C)}=\frac{6480}{14400}=\frac{9}{20}.
\]

Thus
\[
m=9,\quad n=20,\quad m+n=29.
\]

\[
\boxed{29}
\]

Let \(X_i\) be the top face on the \(i\)-th roll.  
Then \((X_1,\dots,X_6)\) is a sequence of 6 independent rolls, each uniformly chosen from 6 faces.

A sticker is visible at the end iff its face is never rolled again later.

We are given the condition:

- sticker \(2\) is visible,
- sticker \(4\) is visible,
- sticker \(6\) is visible.

Call this event \(E\).

---

## 1. Count sequences satisfying \(E\)

For sticker \(2\) to remain visible, its face cannot appear in rolls \(3,4,5,6\).  
For sticker \(4\) to remain visible, its face cannot appear in rolls \(5,6\).  
Sticker \(6\) is always visible.

So:

- \(X_1\): any of 6 choices
- \(X_2\): any of 6 choices
- \(X_3\): anything except \(X_2\), so 5 choices
- \(X_4\): anything except \(X_2\), so 5 choices
- \(X_5\): anything except \(X_2,X_4\), so 4 choices
- \(X_6\): anything except \(X_2,X_4\), so 4 choices

Thus
\[
|E|=6\cdot 6\cdot 5\cdot 5\cdot 4\cdot 4=14400.
\]

---

## 2. Add the requirement that exactly one face is blank

Exactly one face blank means exactly 5 distinct faces appeared among \(X_1,\dots,X_6\).

Since there are 6 rolls and 5 distinct faces, exactly one face value is repeated once, and all others are distinct.

Under event \(E\), some pairs cannot be equal:

- \(X_2\neq X_3,X_4,X_5,X_6\)
- \(X_4\neq X_5,X_6\)

So among the \(\binom{6}{2}=15\) pairs of positions, the forbidden equal pairs are:
\[
(2,3),(2,4),(2,5),(2,6),(4,5),(4,6),
\]
which is 6 forbidden pairs.

Hence the allowed repeated-position pairs are
\[
15-6=9.
\]

For each allowed pair:

- choose the repeated face: \(6\) ways,
- choose the other 4 distinct faces for the other 4 positions: \(5\cdot 4\cdot 3\cdot 2\) ways.

So the number of sequences in \(A\cap E\) is
\[
9\cdot 6\cdot 5\cdot 4\cdot 3\cdot 2=6480.
\]

---

## 3. Conditional probability

\[
p=P(A\mid E)=\frac{|A\cap E|}{|E|}
=\frac{6480}{14400}
=\frac{9}{20}.
\]

Thus
\[
m=9,\qquad n=20,\qquad m+n=29.
\]

\[
\boxed{29}
\]

Let the six faces of the die be \(A,B,C,D,E,F\), and let the roll outcomes be \(r_1,r_2,\dots,r_6\).

After roll \(i\), sticker \(i\) is placed on face \(r_i\).  
A sticker is visible at the end iff that face is never rolled again later.

We are given the condition

\[
E=\{\text{stickers }2,4,6\text{ are visible}\}.
\]

Since sticker \(6\) is always visible, this means:

- sticker \(2\) visible \(\iff r_2\notin \{r_3,r_4,r_5,r_6\}\),
- sticker \(4\) visible \(\iff r_4\notin \{r_5,r_6\}\).

We want

\[
p=P(\text{exactly one face blank}\mid E).
\]

Exactly one face blank means exactly \(5\) distinct faces were rolled among \(r_1,\dots,r_6\).

---

## 1. Count all sequences satisfying \(E\)

Choose rolls in order:

- \(r_1\): \(6\) choices
- \(r_2\): \(6\) choices
- \(r_3\): cannot equal \(r_2\), so \(5\) choices
- \(r_4\): cannot equal \(r_2\), so \(5\) choices
- \(r_5\): cannot equal \(r_2\) or \(r_4\), so \(4\) choices
- \(r_6\): cannot equal \(r_2\) or \(r_4\), so \(4\) choices

Thus

\[
|E|=6\cdot 6\cdot 5\cdot 5\cdot 4\cdot 4=14400.
\]

---

## 2. Count sequences satisfying both \(E\) and “exactly 5 distinct faces”

We split into cases based on \(r_5\) and \(r_6\).

### Case 1: \(r_5\neq r_6\)

Then \(r_2,r_4,r_5,r_6\) are already \(4\) distinct faces.

Number of ways to choose these:

\[
6\cdot 5\cdot 4\cdot 3
\]

for \(r_2,r_4,r_5,r_6\).

Now there are \(2\) unused faces left.

To end with exactly \(5\) distinct faces:

- either \(r_3\) is one of the existing \(3\) faces other than \(r_2\), and \(r_1\) is one of the \(2\) unused faces:
  \[
  3\cdot 2=6
  \]
- or \(r_3\) is one of the \(2\) unused faces, and \(r_1\) is any of the resulting \(5\) used faces:
  \[
  2\cdot 5=10
  \]

Total for this case:

\[
6\cdot 5\cdot 4\cdot 3\cdot (6+10)=360\cdot 16=5760.
\]

---

### Case 2: \(r_5=r_6\)

Then \(r_2,r_4,r_5\) are \(3\) distinct faces.

Number of ways to choose these:

\[
6\cdot 5\cdot 4.
\]

Now \(3\) faces remain unused.

To end with exactly \(5\) distinct faces, \(r_3\) and \(r_1\) must introduce two new distinct faces:

- \(r_3\): \(3\) choices among unused faces
- \(r_1\): \(2\) choices among the remaining unused faces

So this case contributes

\[
6\cdot 5\cdot 4\cdot 3\cdot 2=720.
\]

---

## 3. Compute the conditional probability

\[
|E\cap B|=5760+720=6480,
\]
where \(B\) is the event “exactly one face is blank.”

Thus

\[
p=\frac{|E\cap B|}{|E|}=\frac{6480}{14400}=\frac{9}{20}.
\]

So \(m=9\), \(n=20\), and

\[
m+n=29.
\]

\boxed{29}

Let \(x_i\) be the face on top when Joanne places sticker \(i\).  
Each \(x_i\) is an independent uniform choice from the 6 faces.

At the end, a sticker is visible iff no later sticker was placed on the same face.

We want

\[
p=P(\text{exactly one face blank}\mid \text{all even stickers are visible}).
\]

---

## 1. Describe the condition that all even stickers are visible

The even stickers are \(2,4,6\).

- Sticker \(6\) is always visible, since it is placed last.
- Sticker \(4\) is visible iff no later sticker goes on its face, so
  \[
  x_4\neq x_5,\ x_4\neq x_6.
  \]
- Sticker \(2\) is visible iff no later sticker goes on its face, so
  \[
  x_2\neq x_3,\ x_2\neq x_4,\ x_2\neq x_5,\ x_2\neq x_6.
  \]

Call this event \(E\).

So under \(E\), the restrictions are:

\[
x_2 \notin \{x_3,x_4,x_5,x_6\}, \qquad x_4\notin \{x_5,x_6\}.
\]

---

## 2. Count all outcomes in \(E\)

Choose the top faces in order:

- \(x_1\): \(6\) choices
- \(x_2\): \(6\) choices
- \(x_3\): must differ from \(x_2\), so \(5\) choices
- \(x_4\): must differ from \(x_2\), so \(5\) choices
- \(x_5\): must differ from \(x_2,x_4\), so \(4\) choices
- \(x_6\): must differ from \(x_2,x_4\), so \(4\) choices

Thus

\[
|E|=6\cdot 6\cdot 5\cdot 5\cdot 4\cdot 4=14400.
\]

---

## 3. Interpret “exactly one face left blank”

Exactly one face blank means exactly 5 faces were used among \(x_1,\dots,x_6\).

Since there are 6 placements total, this means:

- exactly one pair of positions chose the same face,
- and the other 4 positions chose distinct faces.

So among the 6 positions, there must be exactly one repeated pair.

---

## 4. Under \(E\), which pairs are allowed to be equal?

Because of the visibility restrictions:

- \(x_2\) cannot equal \(x_3,x_4,x_5,x_6\), but may equal \(x_1\).
- \(x_4\) cannot equal \(x_5,x_6\), but may equal \(x_1,x_3\).

So the allowed equal pairs are:

\[
(1,2),(1,3),(1,4),(1,5),(1,6),
\]
\[
(3,4),(3,5),(3,6),(5,6).
\]

Also \((4,? )\) with \(1,3\) already listed.

That is a total of

\[
10
\]

allowed pairs.

---

## 5. Count outcomes in \(E\) with exactly one blank face

Fix one allowed pair, say positions \(a,b\), to be the unique repeated face.

Then we need 5 distinct faces assigned to:

- the repeated pair \((a,b)\),
- and the remaining 4 single positions.

The number of ways to assign 5 distinct faces to these 5 groups is

\[
6\cdot 5\cdot 4\cdot 3\cdot 2=720.
\]

Since there are 10 possible allowed pairs,

\[
|E\cap B|=10\cdot 720=7200,
\]

where \(B\) is the event “exactly one face is blank.”

---

## 6. Compute the conditional probability

\[
p=\frac{|E\cap B|}{|E|}
=\frac{7200}{14400}
=\frac12.
\]

So \(p=\frac{1}{2}\), hence

\[
m=1,\quad n=2,\quad m+n=3.
\]

\[
\boxed{3}

Let the top face on roll \(i\) be \(X_i\), where each \(X_i\) is independently uniform on the 6 faces.

After all 6 stickers are placed, a sticker is visible iff no later sticker was placed on that same face.

We want
\[
p=P(\text{exactly one face blank}\mid \text{stickers }2,4,6\text{ are visible}).
\]

So we compute:
\[
p=\frac{P(A\cap E)}{P(E)},
\]
where

- \(E\): stickers \(2,4,6\) are all visible,
- \(A\): exactly one face is blank at the end.

Since all \(6^6\) roll sequences are equally likely, we count sequences.

---

## 1. Count sequences in \(E\)

For stickers \(2,4,6\) to be visible:

- \(X_2,X_4,X_6\) must be distinct,
- \(X_3,X_5\neq X_2\),
- \(X_5\neq X_4\).

Count choices:

- \(X_2\): \(6\) choices
- \(X_4\): \(5\) choices (must differ from \(X_2\))
- \(X_6\): \(4\) choices (must differ from both)
- \(X_3\): \(5\) choices (\(\neq X_2\))
- \(X_5\): \(4\) choices (\(\neq X_2,X_4\))
- \(X_1\): \(6\) choices

Thus
\[
|E|=6\cdot 5\cdot 4\cdot 5\cdot 4\cdot 6=14400.
\]

---

## 2. Count sequences in \(A\cap E\)

Under \(E\), the faces \(X_2,X_4,X_6\) are already 3 distinct faces.

For exactly one face to remain blank at the end, exactly 5 faces must have been used total. So among \(X_1,X_3,X_5\), we must introduce exactly 2 new faces beyond \(X_2,X_4,X_6\).

Fix distinct faces
\[
a=X_2,\quad b=X_4,\quad c=X_6.
\]
There are \(6\cdot 5\cdot 4=120\) ways to choose \((a,b,c)\).

Let the other three faces be \(d,e,f\). We need \(X_1,X_3,X_5\) to use exactly two of \(\{d,e,f\}\), with:

- \(X_3\neq a\),
- \(X_5\neq a,b\).

Choose which two new faces appear: \(\binom{3}{2}=3\) ways.

Fix such a pair, say \(u,v\). Count valid triples \((X_1,X_3,X_5)\) using only \(\{a,b,c,u,v\}\), with both \(u\) and \(v\) appearing at least once.

Allowed choices:

- \(X_1\in\{a,b,c,u,v\}\): 5 choices
- \(X_3\in\{b,c,u,v\}\): 4 choices
- \(X_5\in\{c,u,v\}\): 3 choices

Total:
\[
5\cdot 4\cdot 3=60.
\]

Now require both \(u\) and \(v\) to appear:

- missing \(u\): \(4\cdot 3\cdot 2=24\)
- missing \(v\): \(24\)
- missing both: \(3\cdot 2\cdot 1=6\)

By inclusion-exclusion:
\[
60-24-24+6=18.
\]

So for each fixed \((a,b,c)\), the number of valid \((X_1,X_3,X_5)\) is
\[
3\cdot 18=54.
\]

Therefore
\[
|A\cap E|=120\cdot 54=6480.
\]

---

## 3. Compute the conditional probability

\[
p=\frac{|A\cap E|}{|E|}=\frac{6480}{14400}=\frac{9}{20}.
\]

Thus
\[
m=9,\quad n=20,\quad m+n=29.
\]

\[
\boxed{29}
\]

Let \(X_i\) be the face showing on top when Joanne places sticker \(i\), for \(i=1,2,\dots,6\).

Because each roll is fair and independent, each \(X_i\) is independently uniformly distributed among the 6 faces.

A sticker is visible at the end exactly when no later sticker is placed on the same face.

We want
\[
p=P(\text{exactly one blank face}\mid \text{stickers }2,4,6\text{ are visible}).
\]

Let

- \(E\): stickers \(2,4,6\) are visible,
- \(A\): exactly one face is blank at the end.

Then
\[
p=\frac{P(A\cap E)}{P(E)}.
\]

---

## 1. Compute \(P(E)\)

Sticker \(6\) is always visible, since nothing comes after it.

So \(E\) means:

- sticker \(4\) is not covered by \(5\) or \(6\), so
  \[
  X_4\neq X_5,\quad X_4\neq X_6,
  \]
- sticker \(2\) is not covered by \(3,4,5,6\), so
  \[
  X_2\notin\{X_3,X_4,X_5,X_6\}.
  \]

We count valid assignments of \((X_1,\dots,X_6)\).

Since \(X_1\) is unrestricted, we count \((X_2,\dots,X_6)\) first and multiply by 6.

### Case 1: \(X_5=X_6\)

- Choose \(X_5=X_6\): \(6\) ways.
- Then \(X_4\) must differ from that face: \(5\) ways.

Now \(X_3\) can be:

- equal to \(X_5\) or \(X_4\): \(2\) ways, then \(\{X_3,X_4,X_5,X_6\}\) has size \(2\), so \(X_2\) has \(6-2=4\) choices;
- different from both: \(4\) ways, then that set has size \(3\), so \(X_2\) has \(6-3=3\) choices.

So this case gives
\[
6\cdot 5\cdot (2\cdot 4+4\cdot 3)=600.
\]

### Case 2: \(X_5\neq X_6\)

- Choose \((X_5,X_6)\): \(6\cdot 5=30\) ways.
- Then \(X_4\) must differ from both: \(4\) ways.

Now \(X_3\) can be:

- equal to one of \(X_5,X_6,X_4\): \(3\) ways, then \(\{X_3,X_4,X_5,X_6\}\) has size \(3\), so \(X_2\) has \(3\) choices;
- different from all three: \(3\) ways, then that set has size \(4\), so \(X_2\) has \(2\) choices.

So this case gives
\[
30\cdot 4\cdot (3\cdot 3+3\cdot 2)=1800.
\]

Thus
\[
\#E\text{ assignments for }(X_2,\dots,X_6)=600+1800=2400.
\]
Including \(X_1\):
\[
\#E=6\cdot 2400=14400.
\]

So
\[
P(E)=\frac{14400}{6^6}.
\]

---

## 2. Compute \(P(A\cap E)\)

Event \(A\) means exactly one face is blank, so exactly 5 faces are used among the 6 placements.

That means exactly one pair of stickers lands on the same face, and the other 4 stickers land on 4 distinct other faces.

So we need to count which pair can be the repeated pair while still keeping stickers \(2,4,6\) visible.

If stickers \(i<j\) are the repeated pair, then sticker \(i\) gets covered by sticker \(j\). Therefore:

- sticker \(2\) cannot be the earlier sticker in the pair,
- sticker \(4\) cannot be the earlier sticker in the pair.

So valid repeated pairs \((i,j)\) with \(i<j\) are those with \(i\neq 2,4\):

\[
(1,2),(1,3),(1,4),(1,5),(1,6),(3,4),(3,5),(3,6),(5,6).
\]

There are \(9\) such pairs.

For each valid pair:

- choose the repeated face: \(6\) ways,
- assign the remaining 4 stickers to 4 distinct faces among the other 5:
  \[
  5\cdot 4\cdot 3\cdot 2=120
  \]
  ways.

So for each valid pair there are
\[
6\cdot 120=720
\]
assignments.

Hence
\[
\#(A\cap E)=9\cdot 720=6480.
\]

Therefore
\[
P(A\cap E)=\frac{6480}{6^6}.
\]

---

## 3. Compute the conditional probability

\[
p=\frac{P(A\cap E)}{P(E)}
=\frac{6480/6^6}{14400/6^6}
=\frac{6480}{14400}
=\frac{9}{20}.
\]

So
\[
p=\frac{9}{20}.
\]

Thus \(m=9\) and \(n=20\), and
\[
m+n=29.
\]

\[
\boxed{29}
\]

Let the six rolls land on faces \(X_1,X_2,\dots,X_6\), where each \(X_i\) is uniformly chosen from the 6 faces.

Sticker \(i\) is placed on face \(X_i\). A sticker is visible at the end iff that face is never hit again later.

We want
\[
p=P(\text{exactly one face blank}\mid \text{all even stickers visible}).
\]

Let:

- \(E\): all even-numbered stickers \(2,4,6\) are visible.
- \(A\): exactly one face is blank at the end.

Then
\[
p=\frac{P(A\cap E)}{P(E)}.
\]

## 1. Count sequences satisfying \(E\)

Sticker 6 is always visible, since nothing comes after it.

So \(E\) means:

- sticker 2 is visible \(\iff X_2\notin\{X_3,X_4,X_5,X_6\}\),
- sticker 4 is visible \(\iff X_4\notin\{X_5,X_6\}\).

Now count sequences \((X_1,\dots,X_6)\):

- \(X_1\): \(6\) choices
- \(X_2\): \(6\) choices
- \(X_3\): must differ from \(X_2\), so \(5\) choices
- \(X_4\): must differ from \(X_2\), so \(5\) choices
- \(X_5\): must differ from \(X_2\) and \(X_4\), so \(4\) choices
- \(X_6\): must differ from \(X_2\) and \(X_4\), so \(4\) choices

Thus
\[
\#E=6\cdot 6\cdot 5\cdot 5\cdot 4\cdot 4=14400.
\]

---

## 2. Count sequences satisfying \(A\cap E\)

Exactly one face blank means exactly 5 distinct faces appear among \(X_1,\dots,X_6\).

Since there are 6 rolls, this means exactly one pair of positions has the same face, and all other 4 positions are distinct from each other and from that pair.

We must find which repeated-position pairs are allowed under condition \(E\).

### Restrictions from \(E\)

- Since sticker 2 must remain visible, \(X_2\) cannot equal any of \(X_3,X_4,X_5,X_6\).  
  So the only possible repeat involving position 2 is \((1,2)\).

- Since sticker 4 must remain visible, \(X_4\) cannot equal \(X_5\) or \(X_6\).  
  So position 4 can repeat only with position 1 or 3.

Thus the allowed repeated pairs are:
\[
(1,2),(1,3),(1,4),(1,5),(1,6),(3,4),(3,5),(3,6),(5,6).
\]
There are \(9\) such pairs.

### Count assignments for one allowed pair

For any one of these pairs, we need to assign 5 distinct die faces to the 5 resulting “value groups” (the repeated pair counts as one group, and the other 4 positions are singletons).

Number of ways:
\[
6\cdot 5\cdot 4\cdot 3\cdot 2=720.
\]

So
\[
\#(A\cap E)=9\cdot 720=6480.
\]

---

## 3. Compute the conditional probability

\[
p=\frac{\#(A\cap E)}{\#E}=\frac{6480}{14400}=\frac{9}{20}.
\]

Thus
\[
p=\frac{m}{n}=\frac{9}{20},
\]
so
\[
m+n=9+20=29.
\]
